children.name: Human Cognition
children.description: Human Cognition represents the internal psychological, cognitive, and emotional processes that govern how individuals perceive and respond to cybersecurity threats and organizational policies. Guided by behavioral frameworks such as Protection Motivation Theory (PMT) and the Theory of Planned Behavior, this category examines the coping appraisals of personal agency and self/response efficacy (Hanus & Wu, 2016; Bulgurcu et al., 2010), the intrinsic and moral drivers of compliance (Vance et al., 2020), and the cognitive barriers—such as security fatigue, habituation, and optimism biases—that can distort risk perception and impede compliance (Reeves et al., 2021; Skinner et al., 2018).

children.name: Coping and Efficacy
children.description: Coping and Efficacy represents how employees appraise their ability to deal with security threats. Drawing on Protection Motivation Theory, it focuses on self-efficacy (confidence in performing secure actions) and response efficacy (belief in the effectiveness of those actions) to motivate adaptive security behaviors.

children.name: Self-Efficacy
children.description: Self-efficacy refers to an individual’s belief in their own capability to carry out the skills and behaviours required to comply with an Information Security Policy (ISP), including using the right knowledge, judgment, and actions in real situations (Bulgurcu et al., 2010; Pham, 2017). Grounded in Social Cognitive Theory, self-efficacy reflects how confident a person feels in their ability to mobilize the mental effort and resources needed to successfully complete security-related tasks. Within Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), self-efficacy is a core part of the coping appraisal process, meaning it directly influences whether an individual feels capable of responding effectively to a threat (Pham, 2017). Even when employees understand that a risk exists, their willingness to act depends heavily on whether they believe they can actually perform the required protective behaviour. This also affects persistence, especially in high-pressure situations where security decisions need to be made quickly. Research shows that self-efficacy is developed through different types of knowledge and experience. Baral and Arachchilage (2019) identify observational knowledge (learning by watching others), heuristic knowledge (learning from past experiences with similar threats), and structural knowledge (understanding how and why security procedures work) as key contributors. Together, these forms of learning build internal confidence, making employees more likely to correctly identify and respond to threats such as phishing attempts. When self-efficacy is low, employees may experience a sense of futility, where they give up on trying to follow security practices because they believe they are not capable of doing so effectively, even if they understand the risks (Pham, 2017). This is particularly important because Bulgurcu et al. (2010) found that self-efficacy is a strong predictor of an employee’s intention to comply with an ISP. As a result, organizations need to go beyond basic awareness training and focus on building confidence and practical ability, ensuring that intention is more likely to translate into consistent secure behaviour.
children.tldr: An individual's belief and confidence in their capability to execute required security behaviours, which is critical for turning intention into actual secure actions.
children.examples:
- An employee successfully identifying a phishing email after practicing in a safe simulation environment.
- A team member feeling confident in their ability to securely configure their home router after watching a clear, step-by-step instructional video.
actionItems: Incorporate hands-on practice into training to build structural and heuristic knowledge.
Use peer-led demonstrations (observational learning) to show successful security behaviors.
Shift training focus from mere awareness to practical skill-building to reduce feelings of futility.
kpis: Self-reported confidence levels in handling security incidents before and after training
Completion rates of interactive/practical security challenges
Reduction in helpdesk tickets related to basic security procedures

children.name: Response Efficacy
children.description: Response efficacy serves as a vital cognitive mechanism within Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), defined as an individual’s belief that a recommended security response—such as reporting a suspicious email or using multi-factor authentication will be truly effective in mitigating or repelling an identified threat (Hu et al., 2021). While self-efficacy concerns an individual's confidence in their ability to perform a task (an effort-to-action association), response efficacy focuses on the perceived utility or instrumentality of the action itself (an action-to-outcome association). This distinction is critical because employees may be fully assured of their capabilities but can give up trying if they expect their behavior to have no meaningful effect on an unresponsive environment. This state, known as outcome-based futility, identifies a failure in response efficacy rather than a lack of skill or confidence. Empirical evidence demonstrates that coping appraisals specifically response efficacy and self-efficacy are the primary drivers of protection motivation and actual secure behavior, often proving significantly more influential than the fear generated by threat appraisals. For example, research indicates that while perceived threat severity and vulnerability may capture attention, they frequently fail to adopt secure behaviors if the user does not believe the recommended countermeasures work. Consequently, mature Security Education, Training, and Awareness (SETA) programs should transition from "threat awareness" (fear appeals) to "countermeasure awareness," which explicitly demonstrates the instrumental value and ease of specific security tools. By prioritizing these factors, organisations can bridge the "knowing-doing gap" and foster a culture where secure actions are viewed as effective, necessary interventions rather than burdensome, futile mandates.
children.tldr: The belief that recommended security actions (like reporting a threat or using MFA) will actually work to mitigate the threat, moving beyond just fearing the risk.
children.examples:
- A user enabling MFA because they understand it blocks 99% of automated attacks, rather than just doing it for compliance.
- An employee reporting a suspicious email because they see tangible evidence that IT acts on reports quickly to protect the company.
actionItems: Transition training programs from 'fear appeals' (threat awareness) to 'countermeasure awareness'.
Provide clear evidence and statistics showing how specific security tools (e.g., MFA, password managers) directly prevent attacks.
Share success stories where employee reporting directly stopped a potential incident.
kpis: Adoption rate of optional security tools (e.g., password managers)
Percentage of employees reporting simulated phishing emails
Survey scores measuring employee belief in the effectiveness of company security controls

children.name: Personal Agency
children.description:
Personal agency is a foundational mental construct within the Integrated Behaviour Model (IBM) that, alongside attitudes and perceived norms, constitutes an individual’s behavioural intention to act securely. It encompasses the internal conviction that one possesses the power and autonomy to influence security outcomes through deliberate action, a state shaped by underlying emotions and cognitive beliefs (Fertig et al,. 2020). In the security domain, agency is often operationalized as self-efficacy, an employee's judgment of their personal skills, knowledge, and competency to fulfill the requirements of an Information Security Policy (ISP). Empirical research consistently demonstrates that self-efficacy is a primary driver of compliance intentions; employees who feel capable are significantly more likely to adopt protective actions (Bulgurcu et al,. 2010).
Furthermore, the sources highlight a critical distinction between agency-based motivation and fear-based appeals. While traditional programs rely on threat appraisal (fear), higher levels of program maturity are achieved through empowerment, where building a user's confidence in their ability to handle threats (self-efficacy) and their belief in the tools provided (response efficacy) proves far more effective at driving behavior than fear alone. However, personal agency can be undermined by organizational control; if security policies are perceived as overly restrictive, employees may experience psychological reactance. In this state, users view the policy as a threat to their behavioral freedom and may deliberately engage in non-compliance as a way to reassert their own agency (Reeves et al., 2021).
To bridge the "knowing-doing gap," modern frameworks like Human Risk Management (HRM) advocate for a human-centered approach that identifies the root causes of behavior and designs interventions that foster reflective autonomy (Kirova et al., 2018). By transforming users from a perceived "weakest link" into proactive "human firewalls," organizations empower employees to view security as a personal responsibility rather than a mandated burden (Manifavas et al., 2014). Despite its recognized importance in driving sustainable habit change, components of the IBM like personal agency and salience remain significantly under-measured in current security awareness maturity models.
children.tldr: The internal conviction that one has the autonomy and power to influence security outcomes, fostering a sense of personal responsibility rather than mandated compliance.
children.examples:
- An employee proactively suggesting a safer way to share files with a client, rather than simply bypassing a restrictive policy.
- A department taking ownership of their specific security risks instead of viewing it solely as IT's problem.
actionItems: Empower employees by treating them as 'human firewalls' rather than the 'weakest link'.
Avoid overly restrictive policies that trigger psychological reactance; instead, design interventions fostering reflective autonomy.
Involve employees in the creation or refinement of departmental security procedures.
kpis: Number of proactive security improvements suggested by non-IT staff
Reduction in policy workarounds or 'shadow IT' usage
Scores on 'personal responsibility for security' in annual culture surveys

children.name: Behavioural Drivers
children.description: Behavioural Drivers are the psychological motivators and values that determine compliance. This area covers intrinsic motivation, moral beliefs, and counter-neutralization, highlighting how internal ethics and alignment with organizational goals drive secure actions more effectively than external deterrence.

children.name: Intrinsic motivation
children.description:
Intrinsic motivation serves as the primary internal engine for sustainable behavior change, proving significantly more resilient than traditional compliance-driven mandates. This driver is rooted in an internal perceived locus of causality (PLOC), where an individual's actions are guided by personal values and liking rather than external rewards or the threat of sanctions. According to foundational models, this state is shaped by intrinsic benefits, the satisfaction and sense of accomplishment derived from secure actions, contrasted against intrinsic costs like the guilt, shame, or embarrassment associated with non-compliance (Ali et al., 2021).
To cultivate this internal drive, mature programs prioritize empowerment and self-efficacy, ensuring employees possess the "inner confidence" and skills to view themselves as a proactive "human firewall" rather than a vulnerability. A critical mechanism in this process is security valence, the degree to which an employee perceives protecting the firm as an inherently "good" or attractive goal, which serves to prevent psychological distancing from security responsibilities (Burns et al., 2015).
Practical strategies to induce this motivation include delivering personally relevant messaging that links workplace security to an individual's private life, such as protecting their family or personal bank accounts. This approach fosters a sense of reciprocity, where employees feel the organization cares for their well-being and are thus motivated to "return the favor" by protecting organizational assets. Furthermore, gamification and interactive video dramas are leveraged to induce emotional engagement and reinforce desired habits through recognizable peers, making the learning process less daunting and more enjoyable (Barendse, 2023). Ultimately, the shift toward Human Risk Management aims to transform employees into intrinsically motivated stakeholders who view information security as a core personal and moral responsibility.
children.tldr: The internal drive to act securely because it is personally valued and satisfying, producing far more durable behavior change than external rewards or the threat of sanctions.
children.examples:
- An employee reports a suspicious email because catching it feels satisfying, not because a policy demands it.
- Staff apply security habits learned for protecting their family and personal bank accounts to their work accounts.
- Employees volunteer for a security champions programme for the sense of accomplishment and peer recognition, without financial incentives.
actionItems: Frame awareness messaging around personal relevance, linking workplace security to protecting employees' families, finances, and private accounts.
Build self-efficacy through hands-on, skills-based training so employees see themselves as a proactive 'human firewall' rather than a vulnerability.
Use gamification, storytelling, and interactive video dramas to create emotional engagement instead of relying on sanctions and mandates.
Strengthen security valence by communicating why protecting the organization is an inherently worthwhile and shared goal.
Foster reciprocity by visibly investing in employees' personal security well-being (e.g., free password managers or family-focused guidance).
kpis: Voluntary participation rate in optional security activities (champions programmes, drop-in clinics, extra modules).
Attitude and motivation scores from validated questionnaires (e.g., HAIS-Q) measured pre- and post-intervention.
Ratio of proactively self-reported incidents and near misses versus those detected by technical controls.
Engagement with non-mandatory awareness content (opens, completions, repeat visits).

children.name: Moral beliefs
children.description:
Within the psychological perspective of cybersecurity awareness, moral beliefs represent an individual's internal evaluation of whether violating a security policy is fundamentally "wrong," serving as the strongest predictor of compliance intention. Unlike external sanctions, which empirical research often finds statistically insignificant or inconsistent, internalised moral convictions provide a resilient, self-sustaining drive for secure behavior that operates independently of a cost-benefit analysis. This mechanism is rooted in Cognitive Moral Development (CMD), which suggests that rational individuals require a convincing ethical rationale, rather than a simple directive, to fully internalise organizational security goals (Skinner et al., 2018). When security policies are aligned with shared values and a sense of belonging, they are transformed from external mandates into ingrained personal responsibilities that shape an employee's consciousness (Assenza et al., 2020).
To effectively leverage this driver, mature programs must combat moral disengagement, a psychological process where employees distance themselves from the ethical consequences of their actions, often as a coping response to security-related stress or frustration. A primary strategy involves developing counter-neutralization content to actively disarm the "mental tricks" employees use to justify non-compliance, such as "denial of injury" (the belief that no one was hurt) or "appeal to higher loyalties" (prioritising work deadlines over security). By incorporating moral persuasion and highlighting the real-world impact of breaches on the organization's mission, training reinforces an internal moral compass (Tolah et al., 2021). Ultimately, fostering a culture of integrity and accountability ensures that protection is viewed not as a burdensome mandate, but as a fundamental ethical duty supported by peers and leadership role modelling.
children.tldr: An individual's internal conviction that violating security policy is simply wrong, which outperforms sanctions as the strongest predictor of compliance intention.
children.examples:
- An employee refuses to share their login with a colleague under deadline pressure because it feels dishonest, not because they fear being caught.
- Training that shows how a breach harmed real patients or customers, making 'no one gets hurt' justifications untenable.
- A manager openly declines to bypass access controls for convenience, modelling that security is an ethical duty rather than an obstacle.
actionItems: Explain the ethical 'why' behind each policy rather than issuing bare directives, giving employees a convincing moral rationale to internalise.
Develop counter-neutralization content that names and disarms common justifications such as 'denial of injury' and 'appeal to higher loyalties'.
Use moral persuasion in training: highlight the real-world impact of breaches on colleagues, customers, and the organization's mission.
Align security policies with shared organizational values so compliance is framed as a matter of integrity and belonging.
Have leadership visibly role-model secure behavior to reinforce that protection is an ethical duty supported from the top.
kpis: Moral belief and personal responsibility scores from validated questionnaires (e.g., HAIS-Q attitude subscales) tracked over time.
Reduction in policy violations where a deliberate workaround or justification was recorded as the root cause.
Share of employees agreeing that violating security policy is 'wrong regardless of consequences' in culture surveys.
Rate of employees challenging or reporting observed non-compliance by peers.

children.name: Counter-neutralization
children.description: Moral beliefs came out as the strongest predictor in the model, with employees who view policy violations as morally wrong being far less likely to engage in non-compliant behaviour, all while neutralization techniques showed a strong positive effect on a violation intention, meaning that the more employees rely on mental justifications to excuse their actions, the more likely they are to violate policy. Because traditional deterrence strategies are often ineffective, the study emphasizes the need for training that actively counters these rationalisations through targeted counter-neutralization content. This approach involves directly addressing common justifications, such as denial of responsibility (framing security compliance as a matter of personal agency), denial of injury (highlighting the real and tangible consequences of even minor security lapses), and appeals to higher loyalties (reframing compliance as an ethical duty that ultimately supports the organization's long-term goals rather than obstructing them). Based on these findings, the authors recommend that managers design more mature security awareness programs that prioritize moral persuasion over punishment, explicitly identify and dismantle common excuses before they take hold, and carefully leverage anticipated shame as a deterrent, while avoiding the creation of a toxic or blame-driven organizational culture.
children.tldr: Training content that names and dismantles the mental justifications employees use to excuse policy violations, before those excuses take hold.
children.examples:
- A training module that opens with 'It's just one small exception, no one gets hurt' and then walks through a breach that started exactly that way.
- Phishing debriefs that pre-empt 'I was too busy to check' by showing how a ten-second verification fits into real workloads.
- Awareness messaging that reframes 'I bypassed the control to hit my deadline' as undermining, not serving, the organization's long-term goals.
actionItems: Identify the neutralization techniques most common in your organization (via interviews, surveys, and incident root causes) before writing content.
Build training scenarios that voice each excuse (denial of responsibility, denial of injury, appeal to higher loyalties) and explicitly refute it.
Prioritize moral persuasion over punishment: show the tangible harm of 'minor' lapses rather than threatening sanctions.
Use anticipated shame carefully and sparingly, and pair it with a supportive reporting culture to avoid blame-driven toxicity.
Refresh counter-neutralization content regularly as new excuses emerge (e.g., around new tools, AI use, or hybrid work).
kpis: Prevalence of neutralization attitudes (e.g., agreement with 'a small violation is fine if it gets work done') in periodic surveys.
Reduction in policy violations where the root cause was a deliberate justification or workaround.
Pre/post training shift in scenario-based assessments where employees must reject common excuses.
Incident reporting rate maintained or rising alongside declining violations, indicating deterrence without a blame culture.

children.name: Cognitive Barriers
children.description: Cognitive Barriers represent the mental processing limitations that hinder compliant behavior. This sub-category addresses security fatigue, habituation to alerts, optimism biases, and intuitive decision-making shortcuts that distort threat perception and lead to non-compliance.

children.name: Security Fatigue
children.description:
Security fatigue (or cyber fatigue) is a critical cognitive barrier defined as a state of weariness, aversion, or diminished motivation resulting primarily from prior overexposure to cybersecurity demands and training. According to the Four-Component Model, this fatigue is categorised by its source, advice-related (overwhelming or complex information) versus action-related (repetitive or demanding tasks like forced password updates), and its manifestation as either attitudinal (enduring cynicism and moral disengagement) or cognitive (transient mental exhaustion).
The primary cognitive mechanism is depletion, where the effortful act of self-regulation required for secure actions, such as resisting the urge to reuse a password, consumes limited mental resources, eventually leading users to rely on heuristic decision-making and mental shortcuts (Reeves et al., 2021). This is often compounded by habituation, an unconscious process where employees "tune out" repetitive security warnings or advice after reaching a saturation point, essentially filtering out information without conscious attention.
As individuals exceed their compliance budget, they may shut down, ignore essential updates, or begin to view security as "someone else's responsibility," a state closely linked to security-related stress (SRS). To mitigate this, mature programs must limit the number of daily security decisions, automate burdensome tasks through tools like password managers, and ensure that high-risk activities are not scheduled for times when employees are already cognitively exhausted (Dawson, 2019). Ultimately, failing to address this barrier leads to a "knowing-doing gap" where employees understand risks but lack the remaining cognitive resources to act on them.
children.tldr: A state of weariness and diminished motivation caused by overexposure to security demands, which depletes the mental resources needed to act securely.
children.examples:
- Employees clicking through security warnings without reading them after months of near-identical pop-ups.
- Staff reusing passwords late in the day because repeated forced resets have exhausted their willingness to comply.
- A team dismissing new security advice as 'more noise from IT', showing attitudinal cynicism rather than momentary tiredness.
actionItems: Audit and reduce the number of daily security decisions and interruptions employees face (warnings, prompts, mandatory resets).
Automate burdensome tasks with tools such as password managers, SSO, and passkeys so compliance draws on fewer cognitive resources.
Consolidate and simplify security advice; retire redundant or overly complex guidance that drives advice-related fatigue.
Schedule high-risk activities and training away from periods of known cognitive exhaustion (end of day, crunch periods).
Vary the format and timing of warnings and awareness content to counter habituation and 'tuning out'.
kpis: Security fatigue and security-related stress scores from validated survey instruments, tracked over time.
Number of security decisions/prompts per employee per day (before and after reduction efforts).
Warning interaction quality (e.g., time-to-dismiss and click-through rates on security prompts) as a habituation signal.
Adoption rate of automation tools (password manager, SSO/passkey enrolment) that reduce compliance burden.
Late-day versus morning error and violation rates as an indicator of depletion effects.

maturityLevels.level1: Constant interruptions. Policies demand complex passwords rotated frequently. Users frequently bypass controls.
maturityLevels.level5: Frictionless security. SSO and passwordless authentication are standard. Alerts are rare, highly accurate, and actionable.

children.name: Optimism Bias
children.description:
One of the most consistent findings in cybersecurity research is that people systematically underestimate their personal exposure to threats. Optimism bias, the tendency to believe that negative events are more likely to happen to others than to oneself, is not simply a matter of being uninformed. It operates as a psychological filter that causes employees to discount valid warnings even when they are aware of the objective risks (Tsohou et al., 2015 ; Tariq et al., 2014).
The practical consequences are significant. Employees who assume they are unlikely to be targeted are less vigilant, less likely to follow security protocols, and more likely to rely on technical safeguards like antivirus software as a reason to take additional risks (Tariq et al., 2014). Counterintuitively, research using eye-tracking and simulations suggests that optimism can actually increase susceptibility to phishing: employees who are confident in their ability to spot threats pay less careful attention, making them easier to deceive (Jaeger & Eckhardt, 2021).
The bias is not limited to end users. Senior managers are equally susceptible, frequently overestimating their ability to control organisational risk and underestimating vulnerability until a breach makes the threat impossible to ignore. The downstream effect is a reluctance to invest adequately in awareness programmes before an incident forces the issue (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 16; Tariq et al., 2014).
Optimism bias also compounds other cognitive barriers. Combined with hyperbolic discounting, it makes the distant possibility of a breach feel far less real than the immediate convenience of skipping a security step. Combined with confirmation bias, it causes employees to seek out information that confirms they are safe and filter out evidence that suggests otherwise (Tsohou et al., 2015; Moustafa et al., 2021).
Addressing this bias requires more than presenting statistics about threat prevalence. Simulations that give employees a direct, personal experience of falling for an attack are more effective than abstract warnings, because they make the risk feel real rather than theoretical (Moustafa et al., 2021). Vivid storytelling works for the same reason: a concrete narrative is harder for intuitive thinking to dismiss than a percentage figure (Tsohou et al., 2015).
children.tldr: The belief that attacks happen to other people, which filters out valid warnings, lowers vigilance, and paradoxically makes confident employees easier to deceive.
children.examples:
- An employee skims a phishing email carelessly because they are 'good at spotting these', and clicks the link a cautious colleague would have checked.
- Staff taking extra risks online because antivirus is installed, treating the safeguard as permission rather than a backstop.
- A leadership team deferring awareness budget for years because a breach 'has never happened to us', until one does.
actionItems: Run phishing and social engineering simulations that give employees a direct, personal experience of being deceived rather than abstract statistics.
Use vivid, concrete incident narratives from similar roles and organizations so risk is hard for intuitive thinking to dismiss.
Debrief simulation failures individually and non-punitively, explicitly contrasting the employee's expected versus actual performance.
Include senior managers in simulations and share their anonymised results to counter overconfidence at the top.
Frame communications around personal exposure ('this is how you would be targeted') instead of generic organizational threat levels.
kpis: Gap between self-assessed threat-spotting ability and actual simulation performance, tracked per cohort over time.
Perceived personal likelihood of being targeted versus perceived likelihood for colleagues in surveys (the optimism gap).
Simulation failure rate among employees who rate themselves 'confident' or 'very confident'.
Leadership engagement indicators: executive simulation participation and pre-incident awareness budget approvals.

children.name: Habituation
children.description:
Habituation emerged as a key cognitive barrier in the model, marked by reduced responsiveness following repeated exposure, where employees gradually become desensitized to routine security stimuli over time. This effect is especially likely when warnings or training prompts are highly similar, follow predictable patterns, and are not associated with immediate negative consequences when ignored. As a result, individuals may develop "click-through" complacency, filtering out information without conscious attention and thereby weakening situational awareness and deliberate decision-making (Reeves et al., 2021).
To address this, research highlights the importance of polymorphic security warnings that vary in design and timing to activate the brain's natural sensitivity to novelty (Moustafa et al., 2021). Accordingly, organizations are encouraged to shift away from "binge training" approaches and instead adopt "drip-feed learning" strategies combined with intermittent reminders, helping keep security salient while avoiding cognitive fatigue that can lead to disengagement (Skinner et al., 2021).
children.tldr: Desensitization to repeated, predictable security stimuli, causing employees to click through warnings and filter out advice without conscious attention.
children.examples:
- Employees instantly dismissing a browser certificate warning because it looks identical to the hundred they have seen before.
- Annual training completed on autopilot, with staff unable to recall its content days later.
- A phishing banner on every external email being ignored entirely, including on the one message that was actually malicious.
actionItems: Deploy polymorphic warnings that vary in colour, layout, wording, and timing to re-trigger conscious attention.
Replace 'binge training' with drip-feed learning: short, spaced sessions with intermittent reminders.
Reserve interruptive warnings for genuinely high-risk events so routine prompts do not drown out critical ones.
Rotate the format, channel, and messenger of awareness content (posters, videos, team briefings) to sustain novelty.
Attach visible, immediate feedback to ignored warnings (e.g., a follow-up nudge or coaching moment) so dismissal is never consequence-free.
kpis: Click-through and time-to-dismiss rates on security warnings, trended over time as a desensitization signal.
Response-rate difference between novel (polymorphic) and standard warnings in A/B tests.
Knowledge retention scores at 30/60/90 days after drip-feed versus one-off training.
Proportion of genuinely risky prompts acted on correctly (e.g., reported rather than dismissed).

children.name: Cognitive Biases
children.description:
Security awareness training often assumes that if you give people the right information, they will make the right decisions. The research suggests otherwise. Security behaviour is rarely the product of careful, rational analysis. It is shaped by mental shortcuts, heuristics, that operate quickly and largely below conscious awareness, and that systematically lead people toward poor judgements (Tsohou et al., 2015 ; Reeves et al., 2021).
The dual-process model helps explain why. Slow, deliberate thinking can override intuitive responses, but only when people have the cognitive capacity to do so. Under time pressure, distraction, or fatigue, that capacity diminishes and heuristic thinking takes over. Security fatigue is particularly dangerous here: as cognitive load increases, the ability to think carefully about security decisions decreases, and biased responses fill the gap (Reeves et al., 2021).
Several specific biases appear consistently in the literature. Optimism bias leads employees to underestimate their personal exposure to threats, assuming attacks happen to other people or other organisations (Tsohou et al., 2015; Jager & Eckhardt, 2021). The availability heuristic causes people to judge risk by how easily they can recall examples, meaning vivid, heavily publicised threats feel more real than common but less dramatic ones like insider errors (Tsohou et al., 2015). The affect heuristic ties risk perception to emotion: security measures that feel frustrating are perceived as less necessary, while risky behaviours that feel social or helpful are perceived as less dangerous (Tsohou et al., 2015). Anchoring makes it hard to change established habits, such as slightly modifying a weak password rather than replacing it entirely (Reeves et al., 2021). Confirmation bias causes employees who believe they are safe to filter out evidence that suggests otherwise (Tsohou et al., 2015). And hyperbolic discounting consistently leads people to prioritise the immediate convenience of skipping a security step over the distant and uncertain cost of a breach (Tsohou et al., 2015; Moustafa et al., 2021).
The practical implication is that facts and figures alone will not shift these patterns. Interventions need to work with the way people actually think, using vivid storytelling to counter availability bias, realistic simulations to personalise risk and overcome optimism bias, and careful attention to cognitive load to prevent the fatigue that makes heuristic thinking inevitable (Tsohou et al., 2015; Reeves et al., 2021).
children.tldr: Mental shortcuts (optimism bias, availability, anchoring, hyperbolic discounting) that operate below conscious awareness and systematically steer employees toward insecure decisions, especially under pressure or fatigue.
children.examples:
- An employee skips MFA setup because a breach feels distant and unlikely, while the five minutes of effort is immediate (hyperbolic discounting).
- Staff worry intensely about ransomware from the news but ignore the far more common risk of misaddressed emails (availability heuristic).
- A user 'updates' Password1! to Password2!, anchored to the old habit rather than replacing it (anchoring).
- A team convinced 'we're too small to be targeted' dismisses the phishing attempts that reach them as spam (optimism and confirmation bias).
actionItems: Use vivid storytelling and real incident narratives to make common-but-undramatic risks as memorable as headline threats.
Run realistic, personalised simulations so employees experience their own susceptibility and optimism bias is confronted directly.
Reduce cognitive load at decision points (fewer, clearer choices; secure defaults) so deliberate thinking can actually engage.
Design for the shortcut: make the secure path the fastest and most convenient one instead of relying on willpower.
Train staff to recognise named biases in themselves, and time interventions away from high-pressure, high-fatigue periods.
kpis: Perceived personal risk versus perceived organizational risk in surveys, with a narrowing gap indicating reduced optimism bias.
Simulation failure rates under time pressure versus normal conditions, showing how strongly heuristics take over.
Adoption rate of secure defaults and one-click secure options versus manual workarounds.
Pre/post shift in scenario-based judgement tests that probe specific biases (anchoring, discounting, availability).

children.name: Pedagogical Design
children.description: Pedagogical Design focuses on the structural planning, instructional methodologies, and delivery formats of security education, training, and awareness (SETA) programs. It addresses the scheduling and timing of interventions to combat memory decay through continuous, drip-feed, or just-in-time training (Hu et al., 2021; Yeoh et al., 2021), the utilization of active engagement methods like gamification and interactive simulations (Taherdoost, 2024; Bada et al., 2019), and the tailoring of content to specific organizational roles and audience segments to maximize relevance and behavioral change (Alshaikh et al., 2021).

children.name: Delivery Frequency
children.description: Delivery Frequency examines the scheduling and timing of awareness training to sustain knowledge. It focuses on spacing interventions through continuous reinforcement, just-in-time feedback, and drip-feed modules to combat memory decay without causing training fatigue.

children.name: Just In Time Training
children.description: Timing is one of the most underappreciated variables in security awareness training. Just-in-Time (JIT) training is built around a simple insight: rather than scheduling education in advance and hoping it transfers to real behaviour later, JIT triggers training at the exact moment a mistake occurs (Yeoh et al., 2021; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). When an employee clicks a simulated phishing link and is immediately shown what they missed, they are processing that information in the most receptive state possible. The mistake is fresh, the context is live, and the lesson is directly relevant. He and Zhang (2019) put it plainly: educate at the exact moment a mistake is realised, not days later in a follow-up module (p. 253). This approach reduces repeat errors and builds genuine empathy with the consequences of a security failure in a way that abstract training rarely achieves (Yeoh et al., 2021; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). The contrast with annual compliance training is stark. Front-loading information once a year, relying on rote memorisation, and assuming it will remain accessible months later simply does not work (Alshaikh et al., 2021; Yeoh et al., 2021). JIT works because it integrates correction into the moment of failure rather than treating training as a separate event. For phishing specifically, habit formation requires repetition. Yeoh et al. (2021) recommend at least six JIT cycles spaced roughly 28 days apart to build sustained alertness rather than a short-term improvement that fades. The risk to manage is over-exposure. Interventions that are too frequent or predictable tip into habituation, where employees tune out entirely (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Varying format and maintaining realistic conditions are the practical levers for keeping JIT effective over time.
children.tldr: Deliver security training at the exact moment a mistake occurs, capitalizing on high learner receptivity to correct behavior in real-time.
children.examples:
- An employee clicking a simulated phishing link and immediately seeing a landing page explaining the specific clues they missed.
- A user receiving an instant warning alert and brief safety guidance when trying to paste a password into an insecure text field.
actionItems: Integrate automated training triggers with phishing simulation platforms to launch JIT landing pages.
Design JIT learning sequences to run for at least six cycles spaced 28 days apart to form lasting habits.
Rotate simulated scenarios and templates frequently to prevent employee habituation.
kpis: Repeat failure rate on phishing simulations
Average response time of user engagement with JIT feedback
Reduction in click-through rate over six JIT cycles

children.name: Continuous Reinforcement
children.description: A single training event, however well designed, will not produce lasting behaviour change. Continuous reinforcement is the recognition that security awareness is a long-term investment requiring ongoing engagement rather than periodic compliance exercises (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 26). The shift is from intensive "binge training" to frequent, short interventions woven into the rhythm of daily work (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 104). The case for this approach is partly about the forgetting curve. Without regular reinforcement, knowledge decays quickly and the gains from even effective training erode over time. A continuous model with a deliberate reinforcement phase directly counters this, keeping security behaviours active rather than dormant between annual sessions (Sabillon et al., 2019). Micro-learning is the practical mechanism: short, focused interventions that maintain engagement without demanding significant time or attention (He & Zhang, 2019; Taherdoost, 2024). JIT nudges sit alongside this, triggering contextual reinforcement at the moment a risky behaviour occurs, such as attempting to share a confidential file, rather than waiting for the next scheduled session (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 99). Together, these approaches move security awareness beyond a compliance checkbox toward something closer to a culture of ongoing vigilance (Alshaikh et al., 2019; Thamae, 2024). The critical caveat is frequency. There is a tipping point beyond which continuous reinforcement becomes counterproductive. Overly frequent or predictable interventions cause habituation, employees begin filtering them out unconsciously, and the signal is lost in the noise (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Push further and the cognitive load of constant security decisions produces fatigue and, eventually, reactance, where employees deliberately disengage to reclaim a sense of autonomy over their own work (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 9; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 257). The practical target is regularity without predictability, and volume without overwhelm. Varying the format, spacing interventions thoughtfully, and monitoring for signs of disengagement are the levers practitioners have for keeping continuous reinforcement on the right side of that line.
children.tldr: Shift from annual training to frequent, short learning moments that build lasting habits and combat knowledge decay.
children.examples:
- Delivering a 2-minute monthly micro-learning module instead of a single annual 60-minute training session.
- Utilizing automated, context-specific nudges when an employee attempts to send an unencrypted sensitive file.
actionItems: Break down existing annual training modules into brief, monthly micro-learning sessions.
Establish a varied delivery schedule with mixed formats (videos, quick quizzes, nudges) to avoid fatigue.
Deploy automated context-specific nudges at the point of action for high-risk behaviors.
kpis: Long-term security knowledge retention rate over a 12-month period
Monthly participation and completion rate of micro-learning modules
Frequency of insecure behaviors (e.g., sharing unencrypted files) pre- and post-nudging

children.name: Drip-Feed Learning
children.description: The premise of drip-feed learning is straightforward: a little, often, beats a lot, rarely. Rather than delivering security training in large periodic blocks, the drip-feed model breaks content into very short stimulus packages, brief videos, quick quizzes, behavioural nudges, distributed consistently over time (Skinner et al., 2018). Taherdoost (2024) frames this as micro-learning, dividing information into bite-sized units that allow self-paced, focused engagement without overwhelming the learner. The contrast with traditional delivery is sharp. Reeves et al. (2021) liken annual full-day training sessions to cramming for an exam: information gets loaded into short-term memory, but fades quickly once the session ends (p. 13). The same total volume of content, redistributed as 30 two-minute sessions across a year, keeps the topic consistently present rather than intensely visible once and then forgotten. That sustained presence is what builds the kind of top-of-mind awareness that actually influences behaviour in the moment a threat appears (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 13). The habit formation argument is where drip-feed learning makes its strongest case. Behavioural change does not come from a single exposure, however well designed. It comes from repetition spaced over time, reinforcing the same principles until the response becomes instinctive rather than deliberate. Skinner et al. (2018) link this directly to return on investment: intermittent learning produces more permanent behavioural change than massed training, making it not just a pedagogical preference but a practical business case (p. 7). Regular micro-trainings and communications keep security woven into daily working life rather than cordoned off as an annual compliance event (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). For practitioners, the implementation shift is less about content and more about cadence. The question is not what to teach, but how to keep it present.
children.tldr: Deliver training in small, spaced micro-units to build top-of-mind awareness and create permanent security habits.
children.examples:
- Distributing thirty 2-minute interactive video lessons over a year instead of a single 60-minute training session.
- Pushing a quick 1-question security challenge to employee chat applications once a week.
actionItems: Deconstruct long-form training modules into bite-sized stimulus packages under 3 minutes.
Deploy a spaced repetition schedule to reinforce key cybersecurity principles repeatedly.
Establish a micro-learning distribution channel through internal chat platforms or email digests.
kpis: Average monthly training engagement rate
Retention of security concepts tested 3 and 6 months post-exposure
Daily active participation in optional quick-quiz challenges

children.name: Communications Strategy
children.description: Security communication has a frequency problem and a relevance problem, and they need to be solved together. Sending more emails about cybersecurity does not build awareness if employees have learned to ignore them. The shift the literature points to is away from sporadic, compliance-driven notifications toward continuous, multi-channel engagement that treats internal security communication the way a marketer would treat a brand campaign (Alshaikh et al., 2021; Prümmer et al., 2024). The frequency case is well established. Knowledge fades without reinforcement, and e-learning gains in particular tend to erode within six months without regular refreshers (Rantos et al., 2012, p. 264). Micro-trainings at regular intervals, supported by newsletters, blogs, and email communications, keep security consistently present rather than visible once a year and then forgotten (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). Oldham and McAlpine (2019) suggest a practical structure: focus the entire organisation on one specific topic per month, backed by social forum discussions and consistent messaging across channels (p. 114). This drip-feed rhythm allows employees to absorb information at their own pace while staying aligned with the programme's priorities (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 109). Variety is what makes high-frequency communication sustainable. A method mix, drawing on newsletters, posters, screensavers, workshops, floor stickers, and web banners, prevents any single format from becoming invisible through repetition (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 7; Rantos et al., 2012, p. 261). Consistent design and tone across all of these touchpoints matters more than it might seem: visual and tonal coherence builds subconscious recognition, so employees register security messaging even when they are not actively engaging with it (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). Treating the programme like an internal brand, with a name, a visual identity, and campaign materials, significantly improves recognition and recall (Alshaikh et al., 2021; Prümmer et al., 2024). Relevance is the other half of the equation. Frequency without relevance produces noise. Content that connects to employees' personal lives, their families, their home security, their own accounts, earns attention in a way that corporate policy framing does not (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 111). Communication style also needs to flex for cultural context. Employees who operate within hierarchical cultures tend to respond better to authoritative, top-down messaging, while those from more egalitarian backgrounds are more receptive to participatory, peer-level communication (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 15). Getting the channel right matters less than getting the framing right for the audience receiving it.
children.tldr: Run multi-channel security communication like an internal marketing campaign, combining frequency, format variety, and personal relevance to drive recognition.
children.examples:
- Designing a unified visual identity and name (e.g., 'Security Heroes') for all internal awareness materials.
- Running a monthly theme (e.g., 'Phishing Awareness') across emails, digital signs, and intranet banners.
- Adapting training messaging from authoritative directives for hierarchical teams to collaborative tips for egalitarian ones.
actionItems: Establish a cohesive internal brand and design guidelines for all cybersecurity communications.
Create a monthly theme calendar supported by multi-channel assets (digital signage, blogs, newsletters).
Segment communications to include personal safety relevance (e.g., securing home networks or personal email).
kpis: Recall and recognition rate of security campaign messages in surveys
E-mail open rates and link click-through rates for security announcements
Employee adoption rate of personal security tips (e.g. download rate of recommended home antivirus tools)

children.name: Engagement Methods
children.description: Engagement Methods focus on transforming awareness training into an active learning experience. It leverages interactive gamification, hands-on simulations, and storytelling to build emotional investment and translate theoretical security concepts into practical habits.

children.name: Gamification
children.description: Gamification is one of the most practical tools available for tackling a problem every security team knows well: employees who are disengaged, fatigued, or simply tuned out. By applying game mechanics, points, leaderboards, storytelling, and competition, to security training, organisations can shift the experience from something people endure to something they actually engage with (Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 2; Barendse, 2023, p. 7). The motivational case is straightforward. Security consistently gets deprioritised under day-to-day work pressure, so training needs to earn attention rather than demand it. Mechanics like leaderboards tap into natural human drivers, status, competition, the fear of falling behind peers, to create genuine engagement (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 119). For global organisations, gamification has the added advantage of working across cultural boundaries; the language of play tends to translate well (Barendse, 2023, p. 55). Beyond motivation, the learning benefits are significant. Traditional training puts employees in a passive role, reading policies, watching slides, ticking boxes. Gamification flips this by placing users in realistic scenarios where they have to make decisions, not just absorb information. The critical difference is immediate feedback: when someone makes a wrong call in a game, they find out instantly and learn from it, rather than waiting for an annual test result (Barendse, 2023, p. 55; Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 6). This also builds self-efficacy, the confidence to recognise and respond to real threats, which is something passive formats struggle to achieve (Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 1). That said, practitioners should be clear-eyed about where gamification fits. It works best as part of a broader programme, alongside phishing simulations, videos, and communications campaigns, rather than as a standalone solution (Barendse, 2023, p. 32; Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 13). A one-off gamified module will not produce lasting behaviour change; it needs to be embedded in a continuous cycle of reinforcement (Barendse, 2023, p. 83). Quality also matters enormously here. Slapping a progress bar onto a multiple-choice quiz is not gamification in any meaningful sense. The real value comes from well-designed, scenario-based experiences, think escape rooms or branching simulations, that genuinely replicate the decisions employees face (Barendse, 2023, p. 61). There are real limitations to acknowledge. Building high-quality gamified content takes time and money, more so than standard e-learning (Barendse, 2023, p. 110). It also does not land equally with all audiences. Senior leaders and older employees sometimes find game formats patronising or irrelevant, and pushing gamification on a resistant audience can do more harm than good (Barendse, 2023, p. 53; Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 5). Finally, the evidence base has gaps: there is limited long-term research confirming that the behaviour changes gamification produces actually stick over time (Hendrix et al., 2016, p. 58; Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 7). Used thoughtfully, it is a powerful engagement tool, but it is not a silver bullet.
children.tldr: Apply game mechanics and branching scenarios to drive active learning, build self-efficacy, and increase engagement among fatigued employees.
children.examples:
- Designing an interactive 'security escape room' challenge where teams solve puzzles related to physical security and tailgating.
- Implementing a departmental leaderboard showing scores from simulated phishing catches and training module completions.
actionItems: Select and implement game mechanics (points, badges, leaderboards) within the existing learning management system.
Replace passive text quizzes with interactive, branching scenarios or decision-based mini-games.
Conduct target audience segmentation to tailor game elements to different demographics and ensure buy-in from senior leadership.
kpis: Active participation and voluntary completion rates of security training modules
User sentiment and satisfaction ratings regarding training format
Pre- and post-test self-efficacy scores evaluating user confidence in threat detection

children.name: Hands-On Simulations
children.description: If gamification is about making security training engaging, hands-on simulations are about making it real. The core idea is simple: people learn better by doing than by watching or reading. Simulations close the gap between knowing what to do and actually doing it, by placing employees in realistic scenarios where they have to apply security thinking under conditions that mirror actual threats (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 6; Wen et al., 2019, p. 2). The reason traditional methods fall short is not just that they are boring. It is that they separate learning from context. A policy document tells you what phishing is; a simulated phishing email makes you experience it. This "situated learning" approach, where training replicates the environment in which threats actually occur, is what makes simulations effective at transferring knowledge into daily behaviour (Wen et al., 2019, p. 2; Mirkovic et al., 2015, p. 26). The other key ingredient is immediate feedback. When an employee clicks a simulated malicious link and is instantly shown what they missed, the lesson lands in a way that a post-course debrief never could (Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 6; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 119). Simulations come in several forms, and the right choice depends on your audience. Phishing campaigns are the most accessible and widely used, giving organisations a live read on workforce vulnerability while simultaneously delivering training to those who need it most (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 13; Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 7). For technical teams, cyber ranges and wargames, including Capture the Flag exercises, offer a safe environment to practise defence and incident response without putting real systems at risk (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 7; Sabillon, 2021, p. 172). At the higher end, VR environments can achieve retention rates of around 80% for security principles, significantly outperforming standard methods, though the investment required is substantial (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 12). Role-playing scenarios occupy a useful middle ground, assigning employees realistic roles and exposing them to the time pressure and competing priorities that make real threats so easy to miss (Wen et al., 2019, p. 3). The evidence broadly supports simulations as superior to passive methods for improving threat detection (Wen et al., 2019, p. 9; Abawajy, 2014, p. 245). However, practitioners should watch for a few pitfalls. One counterintuitive finding showed that employees who received awareness training were actually more likely to fall for a phishing simulation than untrained peers, possibly due to overconfidence or simply being more active online (Back & Guerette, 2021, p. 440, 443). Habituation is another real risk: if simulations become predictable, employees stop treating them as genuine events and the learning value collapses (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Varying the format, timing, and complexity of simulations is essential to keeping them effective over time.
children.tldr: Bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical behavior through realistic, hands-on simulations that deliver immediate feedback.
children.examples:
- Simulating realistic, targeted phishing emails to measure and train employee vulnerability in real-time.
- Organizing a technical Capture the Flag (CTF) incident response simulation for the IT security team.
- Immersing employees in a VR workspace simulation to identify physical security threats.
actionItems: Deploy regular, varied phishing simulations to general staff with JIT feedback loops.
Set up role-playing scenario workshops for business units, incorporating time-pressure elements.
Vary simulation templates, sender addresses, and timings to prevent user habituation.
kpis: Phishing simulation click-through rate and reporting rate
Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) during simulation exercises
Reduction in repeat-fail rates across simulation cycles

children.name: Storytelling
children.description: Security awareness often fails not because employees lack information, but because the information never connects. Policies get read and forgotten. Compliance modules get clicked through. Storytelling addresses this at the root: it turns abstract security concepts into human experiences that people can actually relate to, remember, and act on (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 116). The mechanism is straightforward. When someone hears a story about a colleague who got a convincing email from what looked like their CEO, they do not just learn a fact about phishing. They feel the plausibility of it. That emotional connection is what drives behaviour change, not the policy that says "verify unusual requests." He and Zhang (2019) put it simply: sharing real experiences makes security material personal, and personal material sticks (p. 251). The goal is to lead learners through a narrative that links to their own background and daily context, so that complex concepts feel grounded rather than abstract (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 118). This is where storytelling becomes especially powerful as a design tool for gamification and simulation. A list of dos and don'ts is easy to ignore. A character navigating a realistic series of security dilemmas, encountering Wi-Fi risks while working remotely, then a suspicious password reset request, then a social engineering call, creates a coherent experience that flows logically from one risk to the next (Barendse, 2023, p. 50, 51). Employees see themselves in those situations. When they return to their actual inboxes, the memory of the character's mistake is more accessible than any bullet point from a training module (Barendse, 2023, p. 50). Placing even a simple quiz within an overarching storyline measurably increases immersion and engagement (Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 7). Video drama takes this further by adding emotional weight. Training built around a recognisable peer, someone who looks and works like the audience, increases self-efficacy: employees are more likely to adopt secure behaviours when they see someone like themselves modelling them (Skinner et al., 2018, p. 4). Crucially, emotion is not just a nice-to-have here. Skinner et al. (2018) argue that it is a fundamental component of rational decision-making, and that video drama can provoke the kind of emotional response that purely text-based training simply cannot (p. 5). Emerging formats like VR-based storytelling are extending this further still, with early evidence suggesting stronger awareness outcomes than traditional e-learning (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 12). For practitioners, the takeaway is this: the format of your content matters less than whether it gives people something to connect with. Characters, conflict, consequence, and context are not creative luxuries. They are the delivery mechanism for the lessons you actually want people to retain.
children.tldr: Use narrative structures, relatable characters, and emotional conflict to make security policies memorable and drive behavior change.
children.examples:
- Creating a video drama series following a fictional employee who falls for a business email compromise (BEC) scam.
- Sharing anonymous, real-world case studies of security incidents that occurred within the company in the monthly newsletter.
- Framing a remote work training module around a day-in-the-life narrative of a traveling sales representative.
actionItems: Integrate character-driven narrative arcs into branching scenario designs.
Produce short, high-production-value video dramas featuring realistic workplace security dilemmas.
Publish internal case studies of real or simulated security incidents to provide organizational context.
kpis: Employee retention of key training takeaways 6 months post-training
Qualitative feedback score on training engagement and relatability
Self-reported changes in security behavior inspired by specific training stories

children.name: Interactive Media
children.description: Interactive media is the broader category that ties together many of the engagement methods already discussed. Serious games, simulations, VR environments, branching scenarios, and even dynamic warning systems all fall under this umbrella. What they share is a common purpose: moving employees from passive recipients of security information to active participants in their own learning (Wen et al., 2019, p. 2; Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 5). The case against passive methods is well established at this point. Lectures, newsletters, and policy documents are not ineffective because they are poorly written. They are ineffective because they ask nothing of the person receiving them. There is no decision to make, no consequence to experience, no feedback to process. Interactive media changes this by requiring employees to engage, analyse, and respond, which is precisely what develops practical skill rather than surface-level awareness (Mirkovic et al., 2015, p. 26). Research backs this up: employees not only prefer interactive formats over text-based ones, they identify threats faster as a result of them (Abawajy, 2014, as cited in Hu et al., 2021, p. 5). One important nuance for practitioners is that interactivity exists on a spectrum, and more is not always better. Extremely rich or complex media can overload users cognitively, making it harder rather than easier to acquire skills if the interface itself becomes an obstacle (Hu et al., 2021, p. 5). The goal is meaningful interaction, not interaction for its own sake. A well-designed branching scenario with clear choices and immediate feedback will outperform a technically impressive VR environment that distracts from the learning objective. The limitations here are worth taking seriously. High-fidelity interactive content, custom games, VR simulations, and cyber ranges, demands significant investment in time and budget that many organisations cannot easily justify (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 7; Barendse, 2023, p. 110). There is also a subtler risk on the other end of the spectrum. Certain interactive formats, particularly polymorphic warnings that constantly demand user decisions, can tip from engagement into fatigue. When employees feel bombarded by required interactions, the result can be "reactance," a psychological pushback where people start deliberately ignoring security prompts in order to reclaim a sense of autonomy over their own work (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 11). This is a useful reminder that the objective of interactive media is to build security habits, not to create friction. Used well, interactive media is among the most effective tools available for security awareness. The practical challenge is matching the format to the audience, the resource envelope, and the specific behaviour you are trying to change.
children.tldr: Implement interactive learning formats (serious games, simulations, branching scenarios) that require active decision-making and provide immediate feedback.
children.examples:
- Implementing branching scenarios where employees choose responses to potential social engineering phone calls.
- Using a serious game interface for policy training rather than static PDFs.
- Deploying dynamic, polymorphic warnings that change appearance to prevent habituation.
actionItems: Select interactive formats that prioritize decision-making over pure multimedia complexity.
Design interface elements that minimize cognitive load to avoid distracting from core security lessons.
Monitor interactive prompts and warnings for signs of user fatigue and reactance.
kpis: Threat identification speed and accuracy in interactive testing
User completion rate and feedback ratings for serious games
Click-through or ignore rate of interactive in-app warnings

children.name: Content Tailoring
children.description: Content Tailoring moves away from generic, one-size-fits-all training by customizing education to distinct organizational segments. It focuses on role-based scenarios, audience risk profiles, and personal life relevance to maximize engagement and behavioral transfer.

children.name: Personal Life Relevance
children.description: One of the most consistent findings in security awareness research is that employees do not ignore training because they are careless. They ignore it because it does not feel relevant to them. It reads as a corporate obligation, something that protects the organisation's assets at the cost of their time. Personal life relevance flips this dynamic by reframing security as something that protects the employee, their family, their finances, and their home, not just the company network (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). The motivational logic is grounded in how people actually perceive risk. Abstract threats to organisational data are hard to feel personally invested in. The possibility that a weak password could expose a family member's bank account, or that a phishing link clicked at home could compromise a child's personal information, is immediate and tangible. Training that targets this personal risk perception consistently produces stronger engagement than compliance-framed alternatives (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). Sebescen (2015) makes this practical: show employees how their personal accounts could be compromised and how that affects their families, and the material becomes relevant in a way that no corporate policy document can match (p. 88). There is also a reciprocity effect worth noting. When an organisation invests in helping employees stay safe at home, it signals that it cares about them as people rather than just as security risks to be managed. Employees tend to respond in kind, becoming more motivated to protect the organisation because the relationship feels mutual (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). In practice, this suggests a specific sequencing approach. He and Zhang (2019) recommend starting with personal online safety topics, safe banking, social media privacy, talking to children about internet safety, before transitioning to workplace-specific content (p. 254). Engagement established through personally relevant material carries over into the professional context far more effectively than starting with corporate policy and hoping it lands. The fact that 84% of employees in one study applied work-based awareness training to reduce risks at home suggests this transfer works in both directions (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 26). The cognitive benefits reinforce the motivational ones. Personal relevance is not just a hook to get people into the training. It is a memory aid. Content that connects to someone's own life, their background, their habits, their family, is simply easier to recall than abstract guidance (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 116, 118). It also helps acclimatise employees to new security habits by anchoring unfamiliar behaviours to familiar personal contexts, which is one of the mechanisms NIST identifies for helping people incorporate new practices into existing behaviour patterns (Tsohou et al., 2008, p. 213). For practitioners, the shift required here is less about content redesign and more about framing. The same underlying security lesson, delivered as a corporate requirement versus as something that protects your family, can produce very different levels of engagement.
children.tldr: Reframes cybersecurity training around protecting personal finances, family, and home networks, boosting motivation and leveraging reciprocity.
children.examples:
- Conducting a webinar for employees on how to secure home Wi-Fi and protect children's online privacy.
- Offering free personal password manager licenses to employees and their immediate families.
- Designing a learning module focusing on identifying scam calls targeting personal bank accounts.
actionItems: Integrate personal cybersecurity tips (e.g. home network defense, identity protection) into the onboarding training curriculum.
Provide resources or tools (e.g. password managers, security check-up checklists) for personal and family use.
Structure training schedules to begin with personal safety concepts before introducing corporate policy requirements.
kpis: Voluntary training completion and engagement rates for home-safety modules
Survey measurement of employee trust and perceived organizational care
Retention of security behaviors that transfer between home and office

children.name: Role based scenarios
children.description: Generic security training has a fundamental credibility problem. When a warehouse operative and a finance director sit through the same module, neither feels like it was made for them, because it wasn't. Role-based scenarios solve this by grounding training in the actual work context of the learner, the threats they are likely to encounter, the decisions they are likely to face, and the access they actually hold (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 136; Thamae, 2024, p. 132). The relevance argument is straightforward. Training that reflects what someone does every day feels credible and actionable. Training that doesn't feels like a compliance exercise to be tolerated and forgotten (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 251). But role-based tailoring is not just about engagement. Different roles carry genuinely different risk profiles, and those differences matter. An employee authorised to transfer funds, a network administrator with elevated access, or an HR manager holding sensitive personnel data, each represents a higher-value target and requires more focused attention than the general workforce (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 169; Dawson, 2019, p. 54). In practice, role-based design requires thinking carefully about context. Barendse (2023) describes a case where training was split between office employees and factory workers, not as an aesthetic choice, but because the threat landscapes were genuinely different. Factory workers dealing with Operational Technology faced risks that had no relevance to a desk-based colleague, and vice versa. The same principle applies across any organisation with diverse functions. Executives deserve particular attention here. They are frequently high-value targets, sometimes made more vulnerable by the preferential treatment they receive, such as being exempted from certain security controls. Role-specific scenarios for this group need to address both their personal exposure and the ways social engineers may impersonate them to manipulate others lower in the hierarchy (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 166). Scholl et al. (2017) offer a useful tiered framework for thinking about depth. General threat recognition is appropriate for all staff. Management and specialist roles warrant deeper content covering privileged access risks and the obligations that come with handling sensitive information (p. 435). This layered approach prevents the twin failure modes of overwhelming non-technical employees and boring technical ones with content that does not match their level. The outcomes associated with role-based approaches are consistently positive. Customised training improves compliance, increases motivation to maintain awareness, and, perhaps most valuably, opens the door to a cultural shift. When security teams sit down with individual business units to discuss their specific risks, the conversation moves from enforcement to enablement, and that changes how employees relate to security altogether (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 11; Thamae, 2024, p. 132; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 252).
children.tldr: Aligns security training scenarios with the specific daily workflows, technologies, risk exposure, and privileges of individual job roles.
children.examples:
- Designing tailored wire transfer fraud scenarios specifically for the finance team.
- Training warehouse or factory personnel on physical security and operational technology (OT) risks.
- Developing C-suite training scenarios that address executive spear-phishing and VIP-impersonation risks.
actionItems: Map organizational departments and roles to identify high-privilege and high-risk groups.
Design distinct, context-specific scenarios for each major group instead of a one-size-fits-all training module.
Integrate management-level risk ownership lessons for C-suite and department leads.
kpis: Reduction in click/compromise rate for role-specific phishing simulations
Training satisfaction and feedback scores segmented by target departments
Incident reporting rates for targeted attack vectors (e.g. BEC reported by finance)

children.name: Audience Segmentation
children.description: Audience segmentation is the foundation that makes everything else in content tailoring actually work. Without it, even well-designed training gets delivered to the wrong people in the wrong way. The core argument in the literature is blunt: a single programme applied uniformly across an organisation is not a neutral choice, it is an active decision to make training less effective (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 7; Alyami et al., 2023, p. 65). The reason segmentation matters goes beyond personalisation for its own sake. Different employee groups carry different risk profiles, hold different levels of access, respond to different communication styles, and bring different cultural assumptions to the table. A programme that does not account for these differences will routinely miss the people who need it most. Alshaikh et al. (2021) frame segmentation as a social marketing principle: by identifying groups that share common attitudes and readiness to change, organisations can apply targeted interventions where they will have the greatest impact (p. 8). The most intuitive starting point is organisational role. The CATRAM model developed by Sabillon et al. (2019) offers a clean four-way split: executives and board members focused on governance; managers leading departmental initiatives; IT staff working with advanced technical content; and end users covering general workplace and personal safety (p. 241). This hierarchy is useful, but it should not be the only lens. Certain functional roles carry disproportionate risk regardless of seniority. Finance teams with wire transfer authority, HR staff with access to personnel data, and executive assistants who effectively hold the keys to senior leadership's systems, all warrant targeted attention that a hierarchy-only model tends to miss (Dawson, 2019, p. 1531; Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 166). Cultural and environmental context adds another layer. Barendse (2023) documents a case where the same organisation needed entirely different content for office staff and factory workers, not just thematically but stylistically. The animated characters that worked well for desk-based employees were met with indifference on the factory floor, where workers dealing with Operational Technology responded better to on-site filmed content with an adventure theme (p. 47). National culture compounds this further: content that resonated strongly in Asia did not land the same way in Africa, and employees from collectivist cultures have been found to approach behaviours like password sharing very differently from those in more individualist contexts (Barendse, 2023, p. 47; Alyami et al., 2023, p. 66). Finally, knowledge level and demographics shape what content is appropriate and how it should be pitched. Segmenting employees by existing proficiency prevents the twin failure modes of boring experts with basics and overwhelming novices with complexity (Hu et al., 2021, p. 755; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 1653). Research has also identified meaningful gender disparities in cybersecurity knowledge, pointing to the need for inclusive programme design that actively addresses those gaps rather than assuming a uniform baseline (Thamae, 2024, p. 129).
children.tldr: Divides the workforce into distinct subgroups based on role-specific risks, communication styles, culture, and proficiency levels to target training impact.
children.examples:
- Grouping employees into specialized pathways: executives, managers, technical IT staff, and general end users.
- Customizing content aesthetics and formats differently for office staff (e.g. digital modules) and factory workers (e.g. on-site adventure themes).
- Developing distinct baseline modules for non-technical novices versus advanced IT experts.
actionItems: Map key target audiences based on access privilege, risk profile, department, and cultural context.
Select tailored delivery formats and training aesthetics that resonate with each specific demographic.
