Assess baseline employee knowledge to establish differentiated proficiency tiers for training paths.
kpis: Training engagement and completion rates segmented by department and demographic group
Incident reporting accuracy among high-value target groups (finance, HR, executive assistants)
Pre- and post-training assessment scores across distinct knowledge baselines

children.name: Organisational Governance & Culture
children.description: Organisational Governance & Culture encompasses the social, strategic, and structural environments that shape security behaviors within an enterprise. It focuses on the role of executive leadership in establishing governance and resource allocation (Sabillon et al., 2017; Flores & Ekstedt, 2016), the cultivation of an organizational culture that promotes security ownership, psychological safety, and positive social norms (Thamae, 2024; Tolah et al., 2021), and the integration of user-centric, accessible policy frameworks that align workforce operations with security objectives (Dawson, 2019; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019).

children.name: Executive Leadership
children.description: Executive Leadership represents top management's role in guiding organizational security. It focuses on setting the corporate tone through visible policy compliance, resource allocation, and active sponsorship to legitimize security as a strategic corporate priority.

children.name: Visible Commitment
children.description: From the Environmental perspective of the business, visible commitment from executive leadership is not just a managerial preference but a foundational driver of effective Information Security Governance (ISG) and a prerequisite for achieving program maturity. This goes beyond passive approval to surround active involvement and role modelling, where senior managers "lead by good example" to personally depict the organization's strategic direction (AlGhamdi et al,. 2020). For instance, when leaders personally participate in security training or simulations, they serve as role models whose behavior is imitated by the rest of the organization (Flores & Ekstedt, 2016). Research consistently indicates that top management support is a primary influencer of employee attitudes; when leaders prioritize security as a shared, core value, they foster an organizational culture of integrity and accountability that motivates employees to take personal responsibility. On the flip side, a lack of visible support or engagement from the board undermines the perceived importance of security, signaling to staff that compliance is a low-priority "tick-box" or "afterthought" exercise (Al Ghamdi et al,. 2020). This organizational apathy often results in insufficient budgetary commitment, with security initiatives being chronically underfunded compared to their actual requirements. Such a deficit can lead to a "false sense of security" among staff, where the absence of recent incidents is mistaken for effective security rather than a lack of current threats. To effectively bridge the "knowing-doing gap," executive leadership must ensure that cybersecurity initiatives are integrated into strategic organizational priorities, providing the necessary financial and human resources to sustain a long-term Human Risk Management (HRM) approach.
children.tldr: Leaders actively role modelling secure behavior, not just approving policy, which signals that security is a core value rather than a tick-box exercise.
children.examples:
- The CEO completes the same phishing simulation as everyone else and shares their result at an all-hands meeting.
- Executives open town halls with a short security moment, treating it like safety briefings in aviation or construction.
- A board that has never mentioned security in company communications, leaving staff to conclude it is an IT afterthought.
actionItems: Have executives personally participate in the same training and simulations as staff, and make that participation visible.
Include security messages in leadership communications (town halls, newsletters) delivered by leaders themselves, not forwarded from IT.
Ensure leaders model the behaviors they mandate: MFA, badge discipline, reporting their own near misses.
Integrate security objectives into strategic planning and hold executives accountable for them alongside business goals.
Counter the 'false sense of security' by having leadership share threat and incident context openly rather than only after breaches.
kpis: Executive participation rate in training and simulations versus general staff rate.
Frequency of leadership-delivered security communications per quarter.
Employee survey scores on 'leadership treats security as a priority' tracked over time.
Security's presence in strategic artefacts: board agenda items and objectives referencing security per cycle.

children.name: Resource allocation
children.description: From the environmental perspective of the business, resource allocation by executive leadership is a foundational critical success factor for Information Security Governance (ISG) and a primary determinant of program maturity. Achieving higher maturity levels requires moving beyond a "check-box" mentality to establish cybersecurity as a strategic organisational priority that receives sufficient, sustained investment in terms of financial funds, skilled personnel, and dedicated training facilities. Crucially, leadership must allocate employee time to ensure security tasks do not compete with daily productivity, as a lack of this resource is a primary barrier to policy compliance. Research highlights that chronic underfunding often stems from a leadership perception of security as a technical "cost centre" rather than a business enabler, with many organisations operating on minimal budgets. To reach higher maturity levels, organisations should adopt long-term budgeting (such as three-year cycles) and justify investments through metrics like Return on Security Investment (ROSI). Furthermore, leadership should facilitate interdisciplinary collaboration, encouraging IT teams to work with HR specialists and industrial-organisational psychologists to leverage specialised expertise in human behaviour and performance analysis (AlGhamdi et al,. 2020).
children.tldr: Sustained investment of budget, skilled people, and protected employee time in security, treating it as a business enabler rather than a cost centre.
children.examples:
- Employees given protected time in work hours for training, instead of squeezing modules between deadlines.
- A three-year awareness budget covering staff, tooling, and facilities, rather than an annual fight for scraps.
- An awareness team that includes an HR specialist and an organisational psychologist alongside IT security staff.
actionItems: Move to multi-year (e.g., three-year) budget cycles for the awareness programme instead of annual discretionary funding.
Justify investment with Return on Security Investment (ROSI) and risk-reduction metrics that speak the board's language.
Explicitly allocate employee time for security tasks and training so compliance never competes with daily productivity targets.
Staff the programme across disciplines: pair IT security with HR specialists and industrial-organisational psychologists.
Benchmark security spend and headcount against peers and maturity targets, and report the gap to the board annually.
kpis: Security awareness budget as a share of IT/total budget, benchmarked against peers and tracked year over year.
Protected training hours allocated per employee per year, and the share actually used.
Budget stability: ratio of approved multi-year funding to ad-hoc, incident-driven spend.
Headcount and disciplinary mix of the awareness function (security, HR, behavioural science).
ROSI or cost-per-incident-avoided estimates reported to leadership each cycle.

children.name: Policy Sponsorship
children.description: Executive leadership sponsorship is essential for giving Information Security Policies (ISP) legitimacy and operational authority within an organization (Dawson, 2019). Active involvement from senior leaders is a key critical success factor for effective Information Security Governance (ISG), ensuring that security is integrated into overall business strategy rather than treated as a purely technical function (AlGhamdi et al., 2020). When leaders are visibly and personally engaged, they signal the importance of security to employees, encouraging greater accountability and individual responsibility for protecting organizational assets (Thamae, 2024). Strong sponsorship also provides the authority and incentives necessary to ensure compliance, preventing policies from being perceived as optional or low-priority tasks (Dawson, 2019). Ultimately, consistent executive involvement fosters a culture of accountability and integrity, helping bridge the gap between policy and practice by embedding security into everyday organizational behavior.
children.tldr: Senior leaders formally backing security policies, giving them the legitimacy, authority, and incentives that stop compliance being seen as optional.
children.examples:
- The Information Security Policy is signed and introduced by the CEO, not published anonymously by the IT department.
- A named executive sponsor champions each major policy, unblocking resources and settling cross-department disputes.
- Policy compliance woven into performance objectives and incentives, backed by leadership rather than delegated to IT enforcement.
actionItems: Have the CEO or accountable executive formally sign, introduce, and periodically re-endorse the Information Security Policy.
Assign a named executive sponsor to each major security policy or initiative with authority to resolve conflicts and unblock resources.
Tie policy compliance to management objectives and incentive structures so accountability sits with the business, not just IT.
Route policy exceptions and waivers through executive review so leaders own the risk they accept.
Report policy adoption and violation trends to the board on a fixed cadence to keep sponsorship active rather than ceremonial.
kpis: Share of security policies with a named, active executive sponsor.
Employee awareness that leadership endorses the policy (survey: 'this policy matters to senior management').
Policy exception volume and average time-to-decision at executive level.
Compliance rate trend following leadership endorsement campaigns versus IT-only communications.

children.name: Organisational Culture
children.description: Organisational Culture shapes the social environment where security habits are formed. It examines security ownership, psychological safety (fostering blame-free reporting), and collective social norms that embed security as a shared, everyday responsibility.

children.name: Security Ownership
children.description: Security ownership is a foundational pillar of organizational culture, reflecting the true maturity and effectiveness of a security program. It refers to the extent to which employees internalize their security responsibilities and demonstrate a proactive willingness to support both their own and the organization's security performance. Research identifies security ownership as highly influential in shaping a resilient security culture, often more impactful than awareness or compliance alone (Tolah, 2021). When effectively developed, it shifts employees from being seen as the "weakest link" to becoming a proactive "human firewall," where information protection is viewed as a shared and personal responsibility rather than a mandated burden (Thamae, 2024). This transformation increases the likelihood that employees will recognize threats, report incidents, and follow policies without constant oversight. Fostering this mindset requires moving beyond top-down enforcement toward leadership that emphasizes trust, role modeling, and shared responsibility. By involving employees in security practices and clearly communicating the value of the information they handle, organizations can encourage intrinsically motivated secure behaviors (Nurse et al., 2025). Ultimately, security ownership helps bridge the gap between knowledge and action, embedding secure practices into everyday organizational routines.
children.tldr: The degree to which employees internalize security as their own responsibility and act on it proactively, without needing constant oversight.
children.examples:
- A team lead pauses a project to fix a risky file-sharing practice their own team invented, without being told to.
- Employees routinely challenge unfamiliar visitors or unexpected access requests because 'that's just what we do here'.
- A department nominates its own security champion and raises risks to IT before audits find them.
actionItems: Involve employees in shaping security practices for their own area instead of handing down finished rules.
Communicate the concrete value of the information each team handles so protecting it feels meaningful, not procedural.
Recognise and celebrate proactive security behavior (reporting, fixing, challenging) at least as visibly as compliance.
Shift managers from enforcement to role modelling: trust-based follow-up rather than surveillance-style oversight.
Create lightweight channels for employees to propose security improvements and show that suggestions lead to change.
kpis: Proportion of incidents and near misses reported voluntarily versus discovered by controls or audits.
Employee survey agreement with 'security is part of my job, not just IT's' tracked over time.
Number of employee-initiated security improvements or champion volunteers per quarter.
Secure behavior observed without prompting in spot checks (clean desks, locked screens, challenged tailgating).

children.name: Psychological Safety
children.description: Psychological safety is a key part of organizational culture because it directly affects how employees interact with security policies and report potential risks. It means creating an environment where people feel comfortable speaking up or admitting mistakes, like clicking on a phishing link, without being blamed or judged. This is important because it allows security teams to get information about issues early, instead of problems being hidden due to fear. In contrast, a blame-focused culture is counterproductive, since employees who feel embarrassed or punished are much less likely to report future incidents. To avoid this, organizations need to reduce fear and make security teams feel approachable and supportive rather than intimidating or overly strict (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). Newer approaches like Human Risk Management (HRM) also highlight that risk shouldn't automatically mean blame. Instead, human behavior should be seen as a normal factor that influences security outcomes. This shifts cybersecurity away from being just a technical issue handled by IT, and instead frames it as a broader organizational responsibility involving leadership, communication, and role modelling. When security expectations are aligned with workplace goals, employees are less likely to feel like they have to choose between being productive and following security rules (Nurse et al., 2025). Overall, building a culture of openness and trust is essential for reducing insider threats and strengthening security. When leadership communicates clearly, encourages feedback, and supports employees, security becomes something people actually care about rather than just another rule to follow. Over time, this helps make secure behaviour part of everyday routines, turning employees into a more proactive and reliable line of defense (Thamae, 2024).
children.tldr: A no-blame environment where employees can admit mistakes and report incidents early, giving security teams information that fear would otherwise bury.
children.examples:
- An employee reports clicking a phishing link within minutes because they know the response will be help, not punishment.
- Incident post-mortems that ask 'what made this mistake easy?' instead of 'who did it?'.
- A security team that runs drop-in clinics and answers questions in plain language, so staff approach them before problems grow.
actionItems: Adopt an explicit no-blame reporting policy for honest mistakes and have leadership state it publicly and repeatedly.
Train security staff to respond to reports with thanks and support first; make the reporter's experience painless.
Run blameless post-incident reviews focused on systemic causes rather than individual fault.
Have leaders and managers share their own security mistakes and near misses to normalise speaking up.
Monitor for punitive drift: audit how reported incidents were actually handled and correct blame-oriented responses.
kpis: Average time from incident occurrence to employee report (shrinking lag indicates growing safety).
Self-reported incidents as a share of all detected incidents.
Survey scores on 'I can admit a security mistake here without fear of blame'.
Repeat-reporting rate: whether employees who reported once report again later.

children.name: Social Norms
children.description:
Social norms within an organizational culture shape how employees actually behave when it comes to security, based on shared expectations and peer influence. These norms are driven by the social pressure individuals feel to follow Information Security Policies (ISP), which is influenced by the behaviour of people around them like managers, coworkers, and leadership. Research shows that these normative beliefs are strong predictors of whether employees will comply, often having a more lasting impact than formal rules or punishments (Bulgurcu et al., 2010). Because employees tend to mirror the actions of those around them, social influence becomes a powerful driver of behaviour, especially when people rely on peers for guidance. When organizations build a sense of collective responsibility, security shifts from being just a technical requirement to a shared value, where employees act as a unified first line of defence.
These norms often exist as unwritten rules like "how things are done here" and can be more influential than formal policies in shaping everyday behaviour. Within the Cybersecurity Culture-Behaviour (CSCB) framework, social norms play a key role in connecting culture to action, as employees are more likely to follow secure practices when they see others doing the same (Sutton & Tompson, 2025). To strengthen this, organizations can promote "security champions" or role models who influence peer behaviour and reinforce positive habits. When secure behaviour becomes the expectation, it helps close the gap between knowing and doing, making security part of the daily routine. Strong, visible support from leadership further reinforces these norms, creating a culture built on accountability and shared responsibility (Ali et al., 2021).
children.tldr: The unwritten 'how things are done here': peer and leadership behavior that predicts compliance more strongly than formal rules or punishments.
children.examples:
- New starters lock their screens within a week because everyone around them does, though no one cited the policy.
- A security champion in each team whose visible habits (verifying requests, reporting phish) get copied by peers.
- A team where sharing passwords is 'normal to get things done', quietly overriding the written policy.
actionItems: Establish a security champions network of respected peers in each team, not just IT appointees.
Communicate positive descriptive norms ('92% of your colleagues report suspicious emails') instead of violation statistics.
Make secure behavior visible: celebrate reports and good catches publicly so employees see peers acting securely.
Identify and dismantle negative unwritten norms (shared logins, badge lending) by fixing the workflow pressure behind them.
Ensure managers model the norms daily, since employees mirror their immediate leaders more than distant policy.
kpis: Survey agreement with 'people around me take security seriously' by team, tracked over time.
Active security champion coverage: share of teams with a trained, engaged champion.
Variance in secure-behavior metrics between teams (converging upward indicates norm spread).
Peer-referral rate: employees seeking security advice from colleagues or champions versus not at all.

children.name: Policy Framework
children.description: Policy Framework covers the administrative structures that integrate security rules into daily operations. It emphasizes user-centric policy design, accessibility, and role-specific ownership to minimize workflow friction and improve workforce compliance.

children.name: Workforce management / integration
children.description: Workforce management and integration takes a system-wide approach to cybersecurity by embedding security policies into every stage of the employee lifecycle, rather than treating them as separate technical requirements (Nurse et al., 2025). This starts at onboarding, where new hires are introduced to security expectations early on and begin to understand their role in protecting organizational assets. As employees move through the organization, more effective approaches shift away from generic training and instead provide role-specific guidance that reflects the different risks and responsibilities across positions, from everyday users to senior leadership (Hu et al., 2021). A key part of making this work is collaboration between technical security teams and departments like HR, allowing organizations to better understand why insecure behaviours happen and design more realistic, practical solutions. Strong frameworks also prioritize inclusivity, ensuring that training and policies are accessible and effective for all employees. Overall, this approach requires aligning security with broader business goals so that policies don't feel like barriers to productivity. When done well, it helps create a culture where secure behaviour is naturally integrated into daily work and seen as a shared responsibility across the organization (Nurse et al., 2025).
children.tldr: Embedding security into every stage of the employee lifecycle, from onboarding to role changes to exit, through HR-security collaboration rather than standalone technical mandates.
children.examples:
- Security expectations covered on day one of onboarding alongside payroll and HR basics, not weeks later as an IT afterthought.
- Access rights and training automatically re-scoped when an employee changes roles, driven by an HR-security joint process.
- HR and security jointly investigating why a team keeps bypassing a control, then redesigning the workflow instead of re-issuing the policy.
actionItems: Map security touchpoints across the full employee lifecycle (hire, role change, promotion, exit) and assign owners for each.
Build security into onboarding from day one, with expectations framed as part of the job rather than an IT add-on.
Create a standing HR-security partnership to analyse behavioural root causes and co-design interventions.
Trigger access reviews and refreshed role-specific training automatically on internal moves and departures.
Audit training and policy materials for inclusivity and accessibility across languages, abilities, and technical levels.
kpis: Share of new hires completing security onboarding within their first week.
Access-rights accuracy after role changes (orphaned or excessive permissions found in periodic reviews).
Time to revoke access at offboarding.
Number of interventions co-designed with HR per year, and their measured behavioural impact.

children.name: Role Specific Ownership
children.description: Role-specific ownership within a security policy framework focuses on moving away from the generic, one-size-fits-all  approaches and instead using targeted, role-based training. This recognizes that different positions come with different cybersecurity risks and responsibilities, so awareness needs to be tailored to what employees actually do in their day-to-day work. By connecting security practices directly to specific job tasks, organizations can close understanding gaps especially for roles that may not regularly deal with sensitive data but are still exposed to risk (Thamae, 2024). Establishing this kind of ownership also means clearly identifying who is responsible for specific security behaviours during the design of any policy or intervention. For behaviour change to stick, organizations need to focus on the actions required from different groups, since these are often tied to particular systems, teams, and workflows (Alshaikh, 2019). A structured, role-based approach ensures that all employees regardless of technical backgrounds receive relevant and practical training. It also allows organizations to measure effectiveness more accurately using tailored metrics, such as department-specific incident trends, rather than relying on broad, generalized indicators (Taherdoost, 2024).
children.tldr: Tailoring security responsibilities and training to what each role actually does, with clearly named owners for each required behavior.
children.examples:
- Finance staff drilled on invoice-fraud and payment-verification scenarios while developers get secure-coding and secrets-handling training.
- A policy that names who verifies wire transfers, who approves access requests, and who reviews logs, instead of 'staff must be vigilant'.
- Receptionists trained on tailgating and visitor handling because that is their actual exposure, not on database hardening.
actionItems: Build a role-to-risk matrix mapping each job family to its specific threats, systems, and required security behaviors.
Replace generic annual training with role-based modules built around each group's real tasks and workflows.
Name an accountable owner for every security behavior a policy requires, at design time rather than after incidents.
Include non-obvious roles (reception, facilities, contractors) whose exposure is real despite limited data access.
Define department-specific metrics and review them with each department's leadership, not just the CISO.
kpis: Share of workforce covered by role-specific (versus generic) training.
Department-level incident and simulation-failure trends compared against that department's tailored baseline.
Percentage of policy-required behaviors with a named accountable owner.
Role-relevance rating of training in post-course feedback by job family.

children.name: User Centric Design
children.description: User-centric design within a policy framework is essential for making cybersecurity practical, understandable, and fully integrated into everyday work rather than something that feels like an external burden. Instead of relying on generic policies, organizations need to develop security guidelines that are directly shaped by how the organization actually operates, including different roles, workflows, and levels of technical knowledge across employees (Ali et al., 2021). This ensures that policies are not only technically accurate but also realistic and usable in real working conditions. A key part of this approach is making policies clear, specific, and action-oriented. Rather than abstract or overly technical language, effective policies should clearly outline what employees are expected to do in specific scenarios, such as handling sensitive data, identifying phishing attempts, or responding to suspicious activity. This reduces ambiguity and helps prevent situations where employees unintentionally fail to comply simply because expectations were unclear or overly complex. At the same time, well-designed user-centric policies reduce cognitive load by making security decisions easier and faster to understand, which increases the likelihood of consistent compliance in high-pressure or fast-paced environments (He & Zhang, 2019). Modern approaches like Human Risk Management (HRM) further strengthen this by emphasizing continuous feedback loops between employees and security teams. Instead of policies being static documents, they become adaptive tools shaped by real user experience, where employees can highlight friction points, misunderstandings, or practical barriers to compliance. This feedback allows organizations to adjust policies to better match actual workflows and different user risk profiles, improving both usability and effectiveness. By prioritizing clarity, usability, and employee input, user-centric policy design helps close the gap between knowing what should be done and actually doing it in practice. This not only improves compliance but also supports a stronger security culture where employees feel more confident and engaged, ultimately transforming them into an active and reliable "human firewall."
children.tldr: Policies written around how people actually work: clear, scenario-based, low-friction, and continuously refined through employee feedback.
children.examples:
- A data-handling policy rewritten as 'if you receive X, do Y' scenarios instead of ten pages of abstract classifications.
- A feedback button on the intranet policy page that routes friction reports straight to the policy owner, with visible fixes.
- Piloting a new acceptable-use policy with frontline teams and revising it after they surface unworkable steps.
actionItems: Rewrite policies as clear, action-oriented guidance tied to concrete scenarios (handling data, spotting phish, reporting activity).
Usability-test policies with real employees under realistic time pressure before publishing them.
Stand up a permanent feedback loop for reporting friction points, and publish what changed as a result.
Review each policy for cognitive load: shorten decision paths, remove jargon, and default the secure option wherever possible.
Version policies iteratively based on user experience and risk profiles instead of only on annual review cycles.
kpis: Policy comprehension scores from scenario quizzes ('what should you do here?') rather than acknowledgment clicks.
Volume of friction reports submitted and percentage resolved with a policy or workflow change.
Unintentional non-compliance rate attributed to unclear expectations in incident root-cause analysis.
Time for an employee to find and understand the correct procedure in usability tests.

children.name: Accessibility
children.description:
Accessibility in a security awareness context means more than making documents available. It means designing policies and training materials so that every employee, regardless of ability, background, or technical literacy, can actually understand and act on them. The literature identifies two dimensions that practitioners need to get right: technical inclusivity and cognitive accessibility (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019).
On the technical side, training materials need to accommodate employees with disabilities. This means aligning with established standards like the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) to ensure compatibility with screen readers and other assistive tools (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). Organisations that overlook this are not just failing a subset of their workforce; they are reducing overall engagement by signalling that the programme was not designed with all employees in mind (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019).
Cognitive accessibility is where most programmes struggle. Security policies are frequently written in the "security alphabet," dense with technical terms and acronyms that mean nothing to the average employee. The result is disengagement, not non-compliance through choice but non-compliance through incomprehension (El-Haddadeh et al., 2012; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). Asfoor et al. (2022) are direct on this point: policies written in complex language cause negligence and stress rather than compliance (p. 277). Simple, jargon-free language is not a stylistic preference; it is a functional requirement.
Vasileiou and Furnell (2019) add a useful structural point. Policies alone are not enough. Employees need guidance that translates abstract requirements into concrete steps: not just "protect your device" but "choose a password at least ten characters long" (p. 10). The distinction between policy and guidance is the difference between telling someone what is required and helping them actually do it.
Finally, accessibility has a cultural dimension. A policy written for one audience will not land the same way with another. Tailoring content to reflect the cultural context and day-to-day reality of different employee groups is what makes a framework genuinely usable rather than theoretically available (Alyami et al., 2023; Jager & Eckhardt, 2021).
children.tldr: Designing policies and training every employee can actually understand and act on: assistive-technology compatible, jargon-free, concrete, and culturally tailored.
children.examples:
- Training videos with captions, transcripts, and screen-reader-compatible modules that meet WCAG standards.
- A policy that says 'choose a password at least ten characters long' instead of 'ensure device credentials meet complexity requirements'.
- Non-compliance in a warehouse team traced to acronym-heavy policies written for office IT staff, fixed with plain-language versions.
actionItems: Audit all training materials and policy portals against WCAG standards, including screen-reader and assistive-tool compatibility.
Rewrite policies in plain, jargon-free language, expanding or eliminating the 'security alphabet' of acronyms.
Pair every policy with practical guidance that translates requirements into concrete steps employees can follow.
Test comprehension with employees of varying technical literacy and cultural backgrounds before publishing.
Localise and culturally adapt content for different employee groups rather than shipping one version for all.
kpis: Share of training and policy materials passing WCAG conformance checks.
Readability scores of policies (e.g., reading-grade level) against a plain-language target.
Comprehension-test pass rates across employee groups with different technical literacy levels.
Non-compliance incidents attributed to misunderstanding in root-cause analysis, trending down.
Engagement rates with training among employees using assistive technologies versus the workforce average.

children.name: Metrics & Impact Measurement
children.description: Metrics & Impact Measurement focuses on the analytical tools and methodologies used to evaluate, track, and benchmark the effectiveness of security awareness initiatives. It incorporates multi-dimensional evaluation methods—including independent observations, phishing simulations, and psychometrically validated questionnaires (Parsons et al., 2017; Scholl et al., 2017)—with direct success indicators like incident reduction, reporting rates, and utility reactions (Manifavas et al., 2014; Alshaikh et al., 2021) to justify security investments and guide maturity progression.

children.name: Evaluation Methods
children.description: Evaluation Methods are the qualitative and quantitative techniques used to track awareness program impact. This includes unobtrusive workspace observations, phishing simulations, and psychometrically validated surveys to gauge employee knowledge and habits.

children.name: Independent Observations
children.description:
Independent observations involve the monitoring of employee behavior regarding information security policies without alerting the subjects, thereby capturing "true" behavior and avoiding the Hawthorne effect (where behavior changes because it is being observed). Independent observations, or "silent monitoring," involve discretion (Rantos et al., 2012, p. 336) to prevent temporary behavioral changes by employees. A primary application of this method is the physical inspection of workspaces. "Conducting walkabouts", as described by Assenza et al., 2020, serve to determine if staff respect basic security rules during the "everyday routine" (p. 277).
Role in the Evaluation Framework
Independent observations are positioned as a critical component of a holistic evaluation strategy, complementing other methods like surveys and automated metrics. While independent observations are often classified as qualitative techniques used to capture "employees' sensation regarding awareness," they can be quantified to provide benchmarks (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260). Assenza et al. (2020) argue that while monitoring security incidents provides a differential indicator, it is reactive. In contrast, observing daily operations through walkabouts offers a measure of the organization's security posture regarding "standard operations" rather than just emergencies (p. 277).
Advantages and Limitations
The sources highlight trade-offs regarding reliability, cost, and organizational culture when using independent observations. Assenza et al. (2020) classify walkabouts and security incident monitoring as having high reliability because they generate unbiased results regarding actual behavior (p. 282). However, Assenza et al. (2020) also note that tools investigating behavior (like walkabouts) are often "more time and resource consuming" compared to methods measuring knowledge or attitude (p. 282).Yet, Scholl et al. (2017) warn that while hidden observations produce realistic results, they may lead to a "more paranoid atmosphere when conducted multiple times" (p. 433). To mitigate legal and cultural risks, they suggest that observations can sometimes be performed in controlled environments, such as role-playing, though this trades some realism for organizational harmony (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 433).
children.tldr: Monitors employee behavior silently and without prior notice to assess everyday policy adherence (e.g. clean desk checks) without triggering the Hawthorne effect.
children.examples:
- Conducting after-hours physical office sweeps ("walkabouts") to check for unlocked workstations or post-it notes containing passwords.
- Reviewing system logs to verify if employees log out of sensitive applications before leaving their desks, without notifying the employees of the audit.
- Observing employee reactions and compliance patterns during standard operations to establish a behavioral baseline, rather than only relying on post-incident audits.
actionItems: Establish clear protocols and guidelines for conducting discreet security "walkabouts" and workspace checks.
Quantify qualitative observations (e.g., counting instances of unlocked screens) to establish behavioral baselines.
Mitigate cultural friction and paranoid atmosphere by occasionally running checks in controlled environments like role-playing or training simulations.
kpis: Percentage of workstations left unlocked or unattended during random physical audits
Rate of clean desk policy compliance (e.g., passwords on sticky notes, sensitive documents left in the open)
Year-over-year reduction in standard-operation security policy infractions discovered via silent audits

children.name: Phishing Simulations
children.description: Phishing simulations occupy a unique position in the evaluation toolkit: they are the closest thing to a real test of whether security awareness training is actually working. Unlike surveys or knowledge checks, which measure what employees say they know, simulations measure what employees do when they think no one is watching. That distinction matters enormously (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 433). The first practical use of simulations is establishing a baseline. Before any training intervention, running a simulation across a range of phishing templates gives organisations a honest picture of where they stand, click rates, reporting rates, and which groups are most vulnerable. Yeoh et al. (2021) describe exactly this approach, using 14 templates to establish an initial click rate of 12% and a reporting rate of 8.3% before training began (p. 10). Without that baseline, it is impossible to demonstrate whether anything has improved. A point worth emphasising: the reporting rate matters as much as the click rate, arguably more. A falling click rate tells you employees are getting better at avoiding mistakes. A rising reporting rate tells you they are actively participating in organisational defence. After six cycles of simulation and embedded training, Yeoh et al. (2021) recorded a reporting rate that climbed from 8% to 35%, which represents a meaningful cultural shift, not just individual improvement (p. 11). For simulations to produce valid results, realism is non-negotiable. Two factors are particularly easy to get wrong. First, contextual relevance: employees are actually less likely to spot suspicious cues when a phishing email closely mirrors their normal work context, such as a convincing HR message arriving during a busy period (Jager & Eckhardt, 2021, p. 5). This means varying the context of simulations is essential to getting an accurate read on vulnerability. Second, time pressure: real phishing attacks exploit busy, distracted people. Simulations that do not replicate this pressure are testing a different, easier version of the problem (Wen et al., 2019, p. 4). The most effective simulation programmes do not just evaluate, they correct. Pairing a simulation with immediate embedded training, a short video or explanatory page triggered the moment someone clicks, turns the evaluation itself into a teachable moment. Yeoh et al. (2021) frame this through operant conditioning: correct behaviour gets reinforced with positive feedback, while a mistake triggers training rather than punishment (p. 7). The correction lands at precisely the moment the employee is most receptive to it (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 119). Two limitations deserve attention. Repeated unannounced simulations can erode trust and create a surveillance atmosphere that damages morale and, in some contexts, raises legal questions (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 433). Predictability is the other risk: if employees begin to recognise simulation patterns, the exercise loses its validity as a behavioural measure (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Varying timing, format, and complexity across cycles is essential to keeping results meaningful.
children.tldr: Measures actual behavior by deploying realistic, simulated phishing emails to track click and active reporting rates under real-world conditions.
children.examples:
- Launching an initial baseline simulation using various templates to record pre-training click and reporting rates.
- Sending targeted simulated phishing emails designed to exploit contextual relevance (e.g. urgent HR messages during busy seasons) or time pressure.
- Triggering immediate educational feedback or modules (such as a 1-minute video) the moment an employee clicks a simulated link.
actionItems: Run multi-template phishing simulations before training to establish honest behavioral baselines.
Design simulations with realistic context, time pressure, and variable complexity across cycles.
Pair simulated failures with immediate, constructive educational feedback rather than punitive measures.
kpis: Simulation click rate (failure rate) across different departments and complexity tiers
Simulation active reporting rate (success rate) to gauge active organizational defense participation
Time taken by employees to report simulated phishing emails to the security operations center (SOC)

children.name: Validated questionnaires
children.description: Most security awareness programmes track completion rates. Fewer track whether the training actually changed anything. Validated questionnaires are one of the more rigorous tools available for closing that gap, offering a standardised way to measure not just what employees know, but how they think about security and how they report behaving (Parsons et al., 2017, p. 41; Assenza et al., 2020, p. 266). The distinction between a validated questionnaire and a standard feedback form is not cosmetic. Validated instruments are built through a structured development process, pilot testing for clarity and length, factor analysis to confirm that questions actually measure the constructs they are supposed to measure, and iterative refinement to remove items that do not perform reliably (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 534; Sutton & Tompson, 2025, p. 104110). The result is a tool that produces consistent, interpretable results over time, rather than a snapshot of how employees felt on a given Tuesday. The most well-known example is the Human Aspects of Information Security Questionnaire (HAIS-Q), designed to measure knowledge, attitude, and behaviour across areas like password management, email use, and incident reporting. Crucially, it has demonstrated predictive validity: employees who score well on the HAIS-Q also perform better in live phishing experiments, which means the questionnaire is not just capturing self-perception but something closer to actual susceptibility (Parsons et al., 2017, p. 43; Moustafa et al., 2021, p. 5). For practitioners, that predictive capability makes validated questionnaires a genuinely useful diagnostic tool, not just a post-training formality. That said, the limitations are real and worth building into any evaluation design. The most significant is social desirability bias: employees tend to give answers that reflect how they think they should behave rather than how they actually behave, particularly on sensitive topics like incident reporting. Parsons et al. (2017) found this effect most pronounced around reporting behaviour, likely shaped by cultural reluctance to be seen calling out colleagues (p. 43). This is not a flaw unique to any particular instrument; it is a structural limitation of self-report methods generally. The knowing-doing gap compounds this. An employee can score highly on a knowledge questionnaire and still click a phishing link under time pressure (Flores & Ekstedt, 2016, p. 26). Questionnaires measure what people know and believe; simulations and observations measure what people do. Neither is sufficient alone, and the most robust evaluation frameworks combine both (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 266; Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260). For organisations without the resources to develop bespoke instruments, the practical answer is to adopt pre-validated scales rather than building from scratch. The development cost is significant; the reuse cost is not (Sutton & Tompson, 2025).
children.tldr: Measures employee knowledge, attitudes, and self-reported behavior using psychometrically validated scales (e.g. HAIS-Q) to predict real-world vulnerability.
children.examples:
- Deploying the pre-validated HAIS-Q questionnaire across the company to baseline employee awareness of password hygiene and incident reporting.
- Comparing survey scores with live phishing simulations to confirm if the survey accurately predicts real-world click susceptibility.
- Adopting pre-validated scales from literature (e.g. Bulgurcu et al.) instead of designing unvalidated internal feedback questionnaires.
actionItems: Select and adopt pre-validated psychometric scales (such as HAIS-Q) rather than developing custom, unvalidated surveys.
Pair questionnaire deployments with actual behavioral metrics (like phishing simulations) to address the knowing-doing gap and social desirability bias.
Conduct anonymous or privacy-preserving surveys to mitigate reporting reluctance and social desirability bias.
kpis: Statistical correlation index between questionnaire scores and simulation performance (predictive validity)
Completion rate and internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) of deployed questionnaires
Average score in the knowledge, attitude, and behavior dimensions of the HAIS-Q or equivalent scale

children.name: Success Measurement
children.description: Success Measurement evaluates the direct operational outcomes of security training. It tracks incident reduction rates, phishing reporting metrics, and post-training utility reactions to demonstrate security ROI and guide program improvement.

children.name: Incident Reduction
children.description: Reducing security incidents is the ultimate goal of any awareness programme, but it is a surprisingly unreliable way to measure whether the programme is working. The relationship between training effectiveness and incident numbers is more complicated than it first appears, and organisations that treat incident reduction as a straightforward success metric risk drawing the wrong conclusions from their own data. The theoretical chain is logical enough: better training produces more compliant behaviour, which produces fewer incidents. The evidence broadly supports this direction of travel. Human factors account for up to 59% of security incidents, but effective training has been shown to reduce employee involvement in incidents to below 5% (Dawson, 2019, p. 2). Scholl et al. (2017) argue that human-caused incident rates are among the most meaningful indicators of awareness programme success (p. 432). The complication is the reporting paradox. When a programme launches and employees become better at recognising suspicious activity, reported incidents typically go up, not down. This is not a failure. Before training, employees did not recognise incidents and therefore did not report them. The initial spike in reported incidents is evidence that the programme is working, not that security is deteriorating (Barendse, 2023, p. 42, 43). Rantos et al. (2012) draw a useful distinction here between confirmed breaches, which should decline over time, and reported incidents, which should rise as vigilance increases, even if many reports turn out to be false positives (p. 332). The long-term trajectory to aim for is a gradual normalisation: reporting rates stabilise as employees develop reliable threat recognition, while confirmed breaches continue to fall (Barendse, 2023, p. 43). The deeper limitation is causality. Even a genuine decline in confirmed incidents cannot be attributed to the awareness programme with certainty. A new firewall, a change in threat actor behaviour, or simply a quiet period could explain the same trend (Taherdoost, 2024, p. 3). The absence of incidents is equally ambiguous: it may reflect strong security culture, or it may simply mean the organisation has not been targeted recently (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 267). Definitional inconsistency adds further noise, since what counts as a reportable incident varies across organisations and over time (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 10). Incident reduction is worth tracking, but it should never be the only measure of programme success. Used alongside behavioural metrics and validated questionnaires, it becomes meaningful. Used alone, it tells an incomplete story.
children.tldr: Tracks the long-term decline in confirmed security breaches alongside a temporary increase in active incident reporting to verify vigilance.
children.examples:
- Monitoring security operations center (SOC) metrics to confirm a long-term reduction in confirmed malware infections.
- Recording the "reporting paradox" where the volume of reported suspicious emails spikes initially post-training before normalizing.
- Analyzing firewall logs and breach data to isolate actual employee-related security failures from external threat trends.
actionItems: Track reported suspicious activity separately from actual confirmed breaches to properly interpret the reporting paradox.
Establish a baseline of incident volumes prior to launching new training campaigns.
Correlate incident volume trends with external threat indicators (e.g. firewall logs, external attack trends) to account for confounding variables.
kpis: Number of confirmed security breaches caused by human behavior
Ratio of false-positive reported incidents to actual confirmed threats (vigilance normalisation)
Year-over-year reduction in cleanup and recovery costs associated with employee-related security incidents

children.name: Behavioural change
children.description: Attendance records and quiz scores are easy to collect, but they do not answer the question that actually matters: did the training change how people behave? The literature is consistent on this point. The chief aim of security awareness programmes is not to inform employees but to change what they do, and success measurement needs to reflect that (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 1631; Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 154). The knowing-doing gap is the core problem. Employees can demonstrate strong knowledge of phishing indicators and still click a suspicious link under time pressure. Behaviour change requires more than information: it requires shifts in attitude and intention, and those are harder to produce and harder to measure (Bada, Sasse & Nurse, 2019, p. 300, 307). Programmes that measure only knowledge acquisition are measuring a precondition for success, not success itself. In practice, phishing simulations provide the most direct behavioural read. Tracking click rates and report rates over time gives organisations a concrete picture of how employee behaviour is shifting. Alshaikh et al. (2021) define success clearly: fewer clicks, more reports (p. 176). Yeoh et al. (2021) frame this through operant conditioning, where a sustained drop in unsafe behaviour combined with a rising reporting rate confirms that the training is producing genuine behavioural change, not just temporary caution (p. 1661). Audit metrics offer a complementary view, tracking the number of employees involved in non-compliant incidents over time and identifying whether shadow security behaviours, where employees bypass policies to get work done, are declining or persisting (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 868; Prümmer et al., 2024, p. 1017). Several frameworks help structure this measurement more rigorously. The Behaviour Change Wheel approach recommended by Alshaikh et al. (2019) frames success in terms of whether the intervention addressed the capability, opportunity, and motivation required to change a specific behaviour (p. 27). The Cybersecurity Culture-Behaviour framework proposed by Sutton and Tompson (2025) goes further, linking cultural values to specific behaviours of concern and recommending organisation-specific measurement tools rather than generic instruments (p. 1239). The honest limitation is that behavioural measurement is genuinely difficult. Most studies track intention rather than actual compliance, long-term retention is hard to assess, and the absence of incidents can reflect a quiet threat environment as easily as it reflects a well-trained workforce (Asfoor et al., 2022, p. 235; Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 1135; Assenza et al., 2020, p. 267). The goal is not a perfect metric but a richer picture, combining behavioural data with cultural indicators and simulation results to build a more honest account of where the programme is actually landing.
children.tldr: Evaluates tangible modifications in employee actions (e.g., clicking fewer simulation links and reporting more threats) rather than just training compliance.
children.examples:
- Deploying the Behavior Change Wheel framework to ensure training targets capability, opportunity, and motivation.
- Auditing clean desk compliance or the frequency of workstation locking through independent observations to measure actual habit changes.
- Correlating drop in phishing simulation click rates with rise in reporting rates to gauge sustainable compliance.
actionItems: Shift focus from attendance tracking to outcome-based actions (e.g. reporting speed, lock screen adherence).
Deploy a cybersecurity culture-behavior framework to link organizational values with specific behaviors of concern.
Identify and reduce "shadow security" practices where employees bypass policies to maintain operational speed.
kpis: Click-to-report ratio in simulated phishing campaigns (lower click rate, higher report rate)
Rate of workstation locks and physical document security compliance observed during random sweeps
Frequency of policy bypasses or "shadow security" workarounds detected in system audits

children.name: Knowledge retention
children.description: Knowledge retention is a reasonable starting point for evaluating a security awareness programme, but a poor finishing point. Knowing that employees absorbed information during training says little about whether they will act on it three months later when a convincing phishing email lands in their inbox. The literature treats retention as a necessary condition for behaviour change, not a sufficient one (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 104). The durability problem is well documented. Post-training tests capture short-term memory, not long-term internalisation. Assenza et al. (2020) note that what is learned in a session may simply not stick, and a test taken immediately after training cannot reveal this (p. 273). Taherdoost (2024) makes the point more sharply: cybersecurity awareness tends to produce immediate, specific, short-term learning unless exercises are repeated consistently over time (p. 2). Security fatigue compounds the problem. As employees are exposed to repeated training and communications, disengagement sets in, and long-term retention becomes harder to achieve and harder to measure (Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 1192). Measuring retention meaningfully requires more than an end-of-module quiz. Longitudinal testing, repeating the same evaluation weeks or months after the original training, is the more rigorous approach, though it introduces logistical challenges around follow-up and participant tracking (Mirkovic et al., 2015, p. 24). Learning Management Systems offer a more practical route, aggregating data on individual and team performance over time to build a picture of how knowledge holds up across the workforce (Skinner et al., 2018, p. 2). Comparative analysis adds another dimension: research cited by Prümmer et al. (2024) found that while instructor-led training produced stronger learning transfer, computer-based training actually delivered higher knowledge retention scores, suggesting the format matters depending on what outcome you are optimising for (p. 1031). The practical implication is straightforward. Retention metrics are worth collecting, but they should be paired with behavioural measures rather than treated as a standalone indicator of success. A workforce that scores well on knowledge checks but continues to click phishing links has not been successfully trained. It has been successfully tested.
children.tldr: Evaluates the long-term durability of security knowledge across the workforce, counteracting memory decay through longitudinal testing.
children.examples:
- Running follow-up quizzes three months after the main training module to measure memory decay.
- Spacing learning elements (drip-feed training) over months to support long-term recall and counteract the forgetting curve.
- Aggregating team-level performance analytics over time via the LMS to identify persistent knowledge gaps.
actionItems: Schedule longitudinal evaluations (e.g. 30, 60, 90 days post-training) to track knowledge decay.
Adopt a spaced-learning or drip-feed delivery model to keep security concepts top-of-mind.
Utilize LMS reporting tools to continuously flag departments with declining retention scores.
kpis: Average score on delayed (longitudinal) knowledge checks vs immediate post-training scores
Percentage of workforce maintaining passing retention scores over a 6-month period
Rate of knowledge decay measured across different training delivery formats (e.g., CBT vs instructor-led)

children.name: Utility reactions
children.description: Most post-training surveys ask whether employees enjoyed the session or found the instructor engaging. Utility reactions measure something more useful: whether employees felt the training was actually relevant to their work. That distinction matters because perceived relevance is one of the stronger predictors of whether learning transfers into behaviour (Griffin, 2011). The concept is straightforward. If an employee leaves a training session thinking "that has nothing to do with my job," they are unlikely to retain or apply what they learned, regardless of how well the content was designed. Griffin (2011) identifies utility reactions as a good predictor of eventual application, skill maintenance, and programme impact over time. This makes them a more functional early indicator of success than satisfaction scores, which tell you how people felt but not whether they will change how they act. The Technology Acceptance Model offers a complementary lens. Reeves et al. (2021) draw on it to argue that perceived usefulness is a primary driver of intention to comply with security behaviours (p. 9). In other words, employees who see security measures as relevant and workable are more likely to follow them. Measuring utility reactions is, in effect, measuring the likelihood of adoption before the behaviour data is available. Alshaikh et al. (2021) frame this through social marketing theory. For behaviour change to occur, the training must present a compelling exchange: the perceived benefits of adopting secure behaviours need to outweigh the perceived costs in time, effort, and disruption (p. 102090). A negative utility reaction signals that this threshold has not been met, and non-compliance is the likely result. Bada, Sasse and Nurse (2019) make the same point through the security-functionality-usability triangle: when security measures feel like obstacles to getting work done, employees route around them (p. 2). In practice, utility reactions are typically measured through questionnaires, though self-report bias is a known limitation and results benefit from triangulation with behavioural data (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 273). The more important practical implication is upstream: if utility reactions are consistently low, the problem is likely relevance, not delivery. The content is not connecting to what employees actually do, and no amount of improved presentation will fix that.
children.tldr: Measures whether employees perceive security training as relevant and useful to their specific daily work tasks, predicting eventual behavior transfer.
children.examples:
- Surveying employees on the perceived usefulness of a module using questions derived from the Technology Acceptance Model.
- Evaluating if employees perceive the time/effort cost of adopting a secure behavior as lower than the security benefits.
- Identifying low relevance scores in specific departments to locate where training content fails to match daily operations.
actionItems: Replace generic "happy sheets" with targeted surveys measuring the perceived utility and work-relevance of training content.
Apply the Technology Acceptance Model to evaluate user intentions to comply based on perceived usefulness and ease of use.
Redesign or tailor modules for departments that report low utility ratings to align with their actual workflows.
kpis: Average rating on the "perceived usefulness" scale in post-training surveys
Correlation index between high utility reaction scores and subsequent simulation performance
Percentage of training feedback indicating that the material was immediately applicable to daily work tasks

children.name: Maturity stages
children.description: Maturity stages provide a structured roadmap to assess and benchmark security program progression. It outlines five levels, helping organizations transition from reactive, compliance-focused training to proactive, data-driven human risk management.

children.name: Level 1: Non-Existent / Reactive
children.description:
At this level, there is no security awareness programme. Not an ineffective one, not an incomplete one. None. Security activity, where it exists at all, is driven entirely by incidents after the fact rather than any proactive intent (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 30; Ridzam et al., 2024, p. 1743).
Employees do not understand organisational security policies, and many do not recognise that they could be targets of an attack. The organisation is broadly vulnerable to human-based threats, with no internal mechanism to detect, report, or correct risky behaviour (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 30). From an audit perspective there is nothing to evaluate: no controls supporting knowledge or attitude, and no evidence of security practice to verify (Ko et al., 2023, p. 185). Where security is addressed at all, it tends to be treated as a technical problem for the IT department rather than an organisational responsibility (Zamani et al., 2021, p. 109).
Progression from this level requires establishing the most basic foundations: defined policies, minimum awareness content, and a clear owner for the function. The priority is not optimisation but existence.
children.tldr: No formal awareness training exists; security is purely reactive, treated as a technical IT issue, and driven only by active incidents.
children.examples:
- Relying entirely on basic firewall and antivirus software without providing security training to the staff.
- Informing employees about security guidelines only in response to a major malware infection or breach.
actionItems: Identify a dedicated owner for the information security awareness function.
Draft and publish basic, mandatory security policies for all personnel.
Establish a direct reporting channel (e.g. abuse inbox) for suspected incidents.
kpis: Percentage of employees who have never received security training
Average time to notify employees of a new security threat

children.name: Level 2: Compliance-Focused
children.description:
At this level, a security awareness programme exists, but its primary driver is regulatory obligation rather than genuine behavioural intent. Training is delivered to satisfy audit requirements, typically through annual computer-based modules applied uniformly across the workforce regardless of role, risk profile, or existing knowledge (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 7; Yeoh et al., 2021).
The limitations are well documented. Annual training relies heavily on rote memorisation, and knowledge decays quickly without reinforcement. Employees tend to experience it as a box to tick rather than something relevant to their daily work, and engagement reflects this (Alshaikh et al., 2021; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 2). Success is measured by completion rates rather than behavioural outcomes, which means the programme can appear effective while changing very little in practice (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 1631).
Progression from this level requires a shift in intent, from satisfying external requirements to genuinely reducing human risk. That means moving toward role-relevant content, more frequent delivery, and metrics that capture behaviour rather than attendance.
children.tldr: Training is driven by regulatory compliance and delivered as a uniform, annual check-the-box exercise.
children.examples:
- Deploying a generic 45-minute annual slideshow to all employees to meet regulatory audit checklists.
- Requiring employees to sign off on a security policy document without interactive comprehension checks.
actionItems: Map regulatory requirements to core training components.
Adopt an LMS platform to track course completions automatically.
Initiate basic phishing simulations to establish a behavioral baseline.
kpis: Training completion rate (%)
LMS compliance audit score
Baseline click rate on simulated phishing emails

children.name: Level 3: Defined & Promoting
children.description:
At this level, the organisation has moved beyond compliance as the sole objective. The programme is structured, documented, and deliberately designed to change behaviour rather than simply inform. Content is beginning to be tailored by role and risk profile, delivery is more frequent than annual, and there is an active effort to promote security awareness as a positive organisational value rather than an imposed obligation (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 251; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 240).
Communication strategies are more intentional at this level, using multiple channels, newsletters, campaigns, micro-trainings, and simulations, to keep security consistently present rather than episodic (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 7; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). Phishing simulations are likely in use, providing a behavioural baseline and enabling just-in-time correction at the moment of failure (Yeoh et al., 2021). Measurement is expanding beyond completion rates toward behavioural indicators such as click rates and reporting trends.
Progression from this level requires deeper integration of security into the fabric of daily work, moving from a programme that promotes good behaviour to one that actively embeds it.
children.tldr: A structured, documented program tailored by role and risk, utilizing ongoing multi-channel campaigns and simulated exercises.
children.examples:
- Distributing short, monthly micro-learning modules instead of a single yearly training block.
- Implementing quarterly phishing simulations and providing immediate feedback to employees who click.
actionItems: Define a 12-month security communications roadmap.
Develop specialized training modules for high-risk roles.
Transition to a continuous phishing simulation program (at least quarterly).
kpis: Frequency of security communications per year
Phishing report rate in simulations
Percentage of employees completing monthly micro-learning modules

children.name: Level 4: Cultural Integration & Behavioral
children.description:
At this level, security awareness has moved from a programme people participate in to a set of behaviours people have internalised. Training is continuous, contextual, and tailored to the specific roles, risk profiles, and cultural contexts of different employee segments (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 8; Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 15). Engagement methods are sophisticated, combining gamification, storytelling, simulations, and personalised content to sustain motivation and build lasting habits (Barendse, 2023; Skinner et al., 2018).
Measurement at this level is multi-dimensional. Validated questionnaires, phishing simulation data, audit metrics, and incident reporting trends are used together to build a rich picture of where the organisation stands (Parsons et al., 2017; Assenza et al., 2020, p. 266). Security is understood as a shared organisational responsibility rather than an IT function, and there is visible leadership commitment that reinforces this framing (Zamani et al., 2021, p. 109).
Progression to the final level requires moving from cultural integration to active optimisation, using data to continuously refine the programme in response to emerging threats and identified gaps.
children.tldr: Security is embedded as an internalized cultural value and a shared responsibility, supported by visible leadership and multi-dimensional metrics.
children.examples:
- Conducting physical office checks and workstation audit sweeps as a standard operational measure.
- Cultivating a blame-free reporting culture where clicking a link is reported immediately without fear of shame.
actionItems: Deploy psychometrically validated surveys (e.g. HAIS-Q).
Ensure executive leaders visibly sponsor and participate in awareness events.
Integrate behavior metrics into standard performance reviews and incentives.
kpis: Average score on psychometric security culture surveys
Rate of active workspace compliance (locked screens, clean desks)
Phishing simulation click-to-report ratio

children.name: Level 5: Optimized & Human Risk Management
children.description:
At this level, the security awareness programme is a continuously improving, data-driven system fully embedded in the organisation's culture and operations. Training runs year-round through varied formats and channels, directly countering the forgetting curve and keeping security genuinely top-of-mind rather than periodically visible (Taherdoost, 2024, p. 9; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 240). The organisation does not wait for incidents to prompt action. Human risk is monitored proactively, content adapts to the evolving threat landscape, and feedback loops drive continuous refinement across every element of the programme (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 268; Thamae, 2024, p. 48).
Measurement is rigorous and demonstrable. Key performance indicators, return on security investment calculations, and documented behavioural trends provide clear evidence of programme impact (Ko et al., 2023, p. 185). Security is understood not as a constraint on productivity but as an enabler of organisational resilience. The goal at this level shifts from improvement to sustainment, maintaining standards while continuing to adapt as both the human and technical risk landscape evolves (Rantos et al., 2012, p. 330).
children.tldr: A continuously optimizing, data-driven system where human risk is proactively managed and training adapts to threat landscape changes.
children.examples:
- Integrating security training tools with real-time SOC security event telemetry to trigger instant educational nudges.
- Dynamically tailoring phishing simulation complexity to match individual employee risk profiles.
actionItems: Integrate security awareness platforms with SIEM/SOC telemetry.
Establish continuous feedback loops to refine policies based on user friction.
Implement adaptive learning paths that adjust training based on threat exposure.
kpis: Reduction in human-caused security incidents (SOC telemetry)
ROI of the program in terms of cleanup cost savings
Accuracy and speed of threat reports from the general workforce
